On the Erdős Covering Problem:
The density of the uncovered set

Paul Balister and Béla Bollobás and Robert Morris and
Julian Sahasrabudhe and Marius Tiba Department of Mathematical Sciences, University of Memphis, Memphis, TN 38152, USA pbalistr@memphis.edu Department of Pure Mathematics and Mathematical Statistics, Wilberforce Road, Cambridge, CB3 0WA, UK, and Department of Mathematical Sciences, University of Memphis, Memphis, TN 38152, USA b.bollobas@dpmms.cam.ac.uk IMPA, Estrada Dona Castorina 110, Jardim Botânico, Rio de Janeiro, 22460-320, Brazil rob@impa.br Peterhouse, Trumpington Street, University of Cambridge, CB2 1RD, UK and IMPA, Estrada Dona Castorina 110, Jardim Botânico, Rio de Janeiro, 22460-320, Brazil julians@impa.br Department of Pure Mathematics and Mathematical Statistics, Wilberforce Road, Cambridge, CB3 0WA, UK mt576@dpmms.cam.ac.uk
Abstract.

Since their introduction by Erdős in 1950, covering systems (that is, finite collections of arithmetic progressions that cover the integers) have been extensively studied, and numerous questions and conjectures have been posed regarding the existence of covering systems with various properties. In particular, Erdős asked if the moduli can be distinct and all arbitrarily large, Erdős and Selfridge asked if the moduli can be distinct and all odd, and Schinzel conjectured that in any covering system there exists a pair of moduli, one of which divides the other.

Another beautiful conjecture, proposed by Erdős and Graham in 1980, states that if the moduli are distinct elements of the interval [n,C​n]𝑛𝐶𝑛[n,Cn], and n𝑛n is sufficiently large, then the density of integers uncovered by the union is bounded below by a constant (depending only on C𝐶C). This conjecture was confirmed (in a strong form) by Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance and Yu in 2007, who moreover asked whether the same conclusion holds if the moduli are distinct and sufficiently large, and ∑i=1k1di<Csuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘1subscript𝑑𝑖𝐶\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{1}{d_{i}}<C. Although, as we shall see, this condition is not sufficiently strong to imply the desired conclusion, as one of the main results of this paper we will give an essentially best possible condition which is sufficient. More precisely, we show that if all of the moduli are sufficiently large, then the union misses a set of density at least e−4​C/2superscript𝑒4𝐶2e^{-4C}/2, where

C=∑i=1kμ​(di)di𝐶superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘𝜇subscript𝑑𝑖subscript𝑑𝑖C=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{\mu(d_{i})}{d_{i}}

and μ𝜇\mu is a multiplicative function defined by μ​(pi)=1+(log⁡p)3+ε/p𝜇superscript𝑝𝑖1superscript𝑝3𝜀𝑝\mu(p^{i})=1+(\log p)^{3+\varepsilon}/p for some ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0. We also show that no such lower bound (i.e., depending only on C𝐶C) on the density of the uncovered set holds when μ​(pi)𝜇superscript𝑝𝑖\mu(p^{i}) is replaced by any function of the form 1+O​(1/p)1𝑂1𝑝1+O(1/p).

Our method has a number of further applications. Most importantly, as our second main theorem, we prove the conjecture of Schinzel stated above, which was made in 1967. We moreover give an alternative (somewhat simpler) proof of a breakthrough result of Hough, who resolved Erdős’ minimum modulus problem, with an improved bound on the smallest difference. Finally, we make further progress on the problem of Erdős and Selfridge.

The first two authors were partially supported by NSF grant DMS 1600742, the third author was partially supported by CNPq (Proc. 303275/2013-8) and FAPERJ (Proc. 201.598/2014), and the fifth author was supported by a Trinity Hall Research Studentship.

1. Introduction

A covering system is a finite collection A1,…,Aksubscript𝐴1…subscript𝐴𝑘A_{1},\dots,A_{k} of arithmetic progressions that cover the integers, i.e., that satisfy ⋃i=1kAi=ℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘subscript𝐴𝑖ℤ\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}A_{i}=\mathbb{Z}. The study of covering systems with distinct differences (or moduli) d1<⋯<dksubscript𝑑1⋯subscript𝑑𝑘d_{1}<\dots<d_{k} was initiated in 1950 by Erdős [3], who used them to answer a question of Romanoff, and posed a number of problems regarding their properties. For example, Erdős [3] asked whether there exist such systems with minimum modulus arbitrarily large, Erdős and Selfridge (see, e.g., [6]) asked if there exists a covering system with all moduli distinct and odd, and Schinzel [10] conjectured that in any covering system there exist a pair of moduli, one of which divides the other. In 1980, Erdős and Graham [5] initiated the study of the density of the uncovered set; in particular, they conjectured that if the (distinct) moduli d1,…,dksubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘d_{1},\dots,d_{k} all lie in the interval [n,C​n]𝑛𝐶𝑛[n,Cn], where n⩾n0​(C)𝑛subscript𝑛0𝐶n\geqslant n_{0}(C) is sufficiently large, then the uncovered set has density at least ε𝜀\varepsilon for some ε=ε​(C)>0𝜀𝜀𝐶0\varepsilon=\varepsilon(C)>0.

The first significant progress on these problems was made by Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance and Yu [7] in 2007, who proved (in a strong form) the conjecture of Erdős and Graham, and took an important step towards solving Erdős’ minimum modulus problem by showing that the sum of reciprocals of the moduli of a covering system with distinct differences grows (quickly) with the minimum modulus. Building on their work, and in a remarkable breakthrough, Hough [8] resolved the minimum modulus problem in 2015, showing that in every covering system with distinct moduli, the minimum modulus is at most 1016superscript101610^{16}. The method of [8] was further refined by Hough and Nielsen [9], who used it to prove that every covering system with distinct differences contains a difference that is divisible by either 2 or 3. However, Hough’s method does not appear to be strong enough to resolve the problem of Erdős and Selfridge, and it moreover gives little information about the density of the uncovered set.

The main aim of this paper is to develop a general method for bounding the density of the uncovered set. Our method, which is based on that of Hough, but is actually somewhat simpler, turns out to be sufficiently powerful and flexible to allow us to also make further progress on the problem of Erdős and Selfridge, and to prove Schinzel’s conjecture. Our starting point is the following natural and beautiful question of Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance and Yu [7].

Question.

Is it true that for each C>0𝐶0C>0, there exist constants M>0𝑀0M>0 and ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0 such that the following holds: for every covering system whose distinct moduli satisfy

d1,…,dk⩾Mand∑i=1k1di<C,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘𝑀andsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘1subscript𝑑𝑖𝐶d_{1},\ldots,d_{k}\geqslant M\qquad\text{and}\qquad\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{1}{d_{i}}<C, (1)

the uncovered set has density at least ε𝜀\varepsilon?

In Section 10, below, we will answer this question negatively for every C⩾1𝐶1C\geqslant 1, by constructing (a sequence of) families of arithmetic progressions with arbitrarily large moduli, for which the density of the uncovered set is arbitrarily small, and ∑i=1k1di<1superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘1subscript𝑑𝑖1\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{1}{d_{i}}<1. However, this immediately suggests the following question: what condition on the (distinct) moduli d1,…,dksubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘d_{1},\ldots,d_{k}, in place of (1), would allow us to deduce a lower bound (depending only on C𝐶C) on the density of the uncovered set? Our main theorem provides a sufficient condition that is close to best possible.

Theorem 1.1.

Let ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0 and let μ𝜇\mu be the multiplicative function defined by

μ​(pi)=1+(log⁡p)3+εp𝜇superscript𝑝𝑖1superscript𝑝3𝜀𝑝\mu(p^{i})=1+\frac{(\log p)^{3+\varepsilon}}{p} (2)

for all primes p𝑝p and integers i⩾1𝑖1i\geqslant 1. There exists M>0𝑀0M>0 so that if A1,…,Aksubscript𝐴1…subscript𝐴𝑘A_{1},\dots,A_{k} are arithmetic progressions with distinct moduli d1,…,dk⩾Msubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘𝑀d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\geqslant M, and

C=∑i=1kμ​(di)di,𝐶superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘𝜇subscript𝑑𝑖subscript𝑑𝑖C=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{\mu(d_{i})}{d_{i}},

then the density of the uncovered set R:=ℤ∖⋃i=1kAiassign𝑅ℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘subscript𝐴𝑖R:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}A_{i} is at least e−4​C/2superscript𝑒4𝐶2e^{-4C}/2.

Note that Hough’s theorem is an immediate consequence of Theorem 1.1. Our proof of Theorem 1.1 was inspired by that of Hough [8], but is simpler in various important ways (for example, we do not need to appeal to the Lovász Local Lemma, and do not need his notion of quasi-randomness), and as a result we obtain a somewhat simpler proof of his theorem, with a better bound on the minimum difference (less than 106superscript10610^{6}). Our method of sieving, which (as we shall see) has a number of further applications, is outlined in Section 2.

We remark that the question of Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance and Yu [7] corresponds to replacing μ𝜇\mu by the constant function 1. As noted above, we will show that the conclusion of the theorem does not hold under this weaker condition; in fact, we will prove that the theorem is close to best possible in the following much stronger sense. We will show (see Section 10) that if (2) is replaced by

μ​(pi)=1+λp𝜇superscript𝑝𝑖1𝜆𝑝\mu(p^{i})=1+\frac{\lambda}{p}

for any fixed λ>0𝜆0\lambda>0, then there exists a constant C=C​(λ)>0𝐶𝐶𝜆0C=C(\lambda)>0 such that the following holds: for every M>0𝑀0M>0 and ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0, there exists a finite collection of arithmetic progressions, with distinct moduli d1,…,dk⩾Msubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘𝑀d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\geqslant M satisfying ∑i=1kμ​(di)di⩽Csuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘𝜇subscript𝑑𝑖subscript𝑑𝑖𝐶\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{\mu(d_{i})}{d_{i}}\leqslant C, such that the uncovered set has density less than ε𝜀\varepsilon. It would be extremely interesting to characterize the functions μ𝜇\mu such that, under the conditions of Theorem 1.1, the density of the uncovered set is bounded from below by a constant δ​(C)>0𝛿𝐶0\delta(C)>0 depending only on C𝐶C.

Although our sieve was developed to control the density of the uncovered set, it turns out that it can be used to prove a number of additional interesting results about covering systems. We will focus here on the two classical examples mentioned above: the question of Erdős and Selfridge, and the conjecture of Schinzel. Over 50 years ago, Erdős and Selfridge (see [6] or [10]) asked whether or not there exist covering systems with distinct odd moduli.111Moreover, as recounted in [6], Erdős (who thought that such coverings are likely to exist) offered $25 for a proof that there is no covering with these properties, and Selfridge (who expected the opposite) offered $300 (later increased to $2000) for a construction of such a covering. Schinzel [10] showed that if no such covering system exists, then for every polynomial f​(x)∈ℤ​[X]𝑓𝑥ℤdelimited-[]𝑋f(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[X] with f≢1not-equivalent-to𝑓1f\not\equiv 1, f​(0)≠0𝑓00f(0)\neq 0 and f​(1)≠−1𝑓11f(1)\neq-1, there exists an (infinite) arithmetic progression of values of n∈ℤ𝑛ℤn\in\mathbb{Z} such that xn+f​(x)superscript𝑥𝑛𝑓𝑥x^{n}+f(x) is irreducible over the rationals. He also showed that this would imply the following statement: in any covering system, one of the moduli divides another. In Section 9 we will prove this latter statement, known as Schinzel’s conjecture.

Theorem 1.2.

If 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} is a finite collection of arithmetic progressions that covers the integers, then at least one of the moduli divides another.

Unfortunately, our method does not seem to be strong enough to resolve the Erdős–Selfridge problem (see the discussion in Section 6). However, it does allow us to make some further progress towards a solution; in particular, we can prove that no such covering system exists under the additional constraint that the moduli are square-free. Since this application of our sieve requires several additional (somewhat technical) ideas, we will give the details elsewhere [1].

Conjecture 1.3.

If 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} is a finite collection of arithmetic progressions with distinct square-free moduli that covers the integers, then at least one of the moduli is even.

A different strengthening of the condition in the Erdős–Selfridge problem was considered recently by Hough and Nielsen [9], who showed that in any covering system with distinct moduli, one of the moduli is divisible by either 222 or 333. Their proof required careful optimization of their techniques, and it seems difficult to use it to strengthen their result. Using our methods, we will give a short proof of the following strenthening of their theorem.

Theorem 1.4.

Let 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} be a finite collection of arithmetic progressions with distinct moduli that covers the integers, and let Q=lcm⁡(D)𝑄lcm𝐷Q=\operatorname{lcm}(D) be the least common multiple of the moduli. Then either 2∣Qconditional2𝑄2\mid Q, or 9∣Qconditional9𝑄9\mid Q, or 15∣Qconditional15𝑄15\mid Q.

In other words, either there is an even d𝑑d, a d𝑑d divisible by 32=9superscript3293^{2}=9, or there are d1,d2∈Dsubscript𝑑1subscript𝑑2𝐷d_{1},d_{2}\in D (possibly equal) with 3∣d1conditional3subscript𝑑13\mid d_{1} and 5∣d2conditional5subscript𝑑25\mid d_{2}. We remark that we are unable to prove that a single d∈D𝑑𝐷d\in D has 15∣dconditional15𝑑15\mid d in this last case.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows: in Section 2 we outline the sieve we will use in the proofs, and in Section 3 we state and prove our main technical results, Theorems 3.1 and 3.2. In Section 4 we complete the proof of Theorem 1.1, and in Section 5 we prove a variant of the main result of [7]. In Section 6 we explain how our sieve can be used to prove the non-existence of coverings sets with certain properties, and in Sections 7–9 we use this method to improve Hough’s bound on the minimum modulus, and to prove Theorems 1.2 and 1.4. Finally, in Section 10, we provide the constructions described above.

2. The Sieve

In this section we will outline the proof of Theorem 1.1. We consider a finite set of moduli denoted by D⊆ℕ𝐷ℕD\subseteq\mathbb{N} and a finite collection 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} of arithmetic progressions, where Ad=ad+d​ℤsubscript𝐴𝑑subscript𝑎𝑑𝑑ℤA_{d}=a_{d}+d\mathbb{Z} is an arithmetic progression with modulus d𝑑d. The goal is to estimate the density of the uncovered set

R:=ℤ∖⋃d∈DAd.assign𝑅ℤsubscript𝑑𝐷subscript𝐴𝑑R:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{d\in D}A_{d}.

Rather than considering the entire collection of progressions 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} all at once, we expose the progressions ‘prime by prime’ and track how the density of the uncovered set evolves. To be more precise, let p1subscript𝑝1p_{1}, p2subscript𝑝2p_{2}, …, pnsubscript𝑝𝑛p_{n} be the distinct prime factors of Q:=lcm⁡(D)assign𝑄lcm𝐷Q:=\operatorname{lcm}(D) (usually, but not necessarily, listed in increasing order) so that

Q=∏j=1npjγj𝑄superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑗1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗subscript𝛾𝑗Q=\prod_{j=1}^{n}p_{j}^{\gamma_{j}}

for some integers γj⩾1subscript𝛾𝑗1\gamma_{j}\geqslant 1. Define, for each 0⩽i⩽n0𝑖𝑛0\leqslant i\leqslant n,

Qi:=∏j=1ipjγjassignsubscript𝑄𝑖superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑗1𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗subscript𝛾𝑗Q_{i}:=\prod_{j=1}^{i}p_{j}^{\gamma_{j}}

and write

Di:={d∈D:d∣Qi}and𝒜i:={Ad:d∈Di}formulae-sequenceassignsubscript𝐷𝑖conditional-set𝑑𝐷conditional𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖andassignsubscript𝒜𝑖conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖D_{i}:=\big{\{}d\in D:d\mid Q_{i}\big{\}}\qquad\text{and}\qquad\mathcal{A}_{i}:=\big{\{}A_{d}:d\in D_{i}\big{\}}

for the set of differences and the corresponding arithmetic progressions whose prime factorization only includes the first i𝑖i of these primes. (In particular, Q0=1subscript𝑄01Q_{0}=1 and D0=𝒜0=∅subscript𝐷0subscript𝒜0D_{0}=\mathcal{A}_{0}=\emptyset.) Note that, although lcm⁡(Di)∣Qiconditionallcmsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑄𝑖\operatorname{lcm}(D_{i})\mid Q_{i}, we do not necessarily have lcm⁡(Di)=Qilcmsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑄𝑖\operatorname{lcm}(D_{i})=Q_{i}, since earlier primes can occur to higher powers in later moduli. Let

Ri:=ℤ∖⋃d∈DiAd=ℤ∖⋃Ad∈𝒜iAd,assignsubscript𝑅𝑖ℤsubscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝐴𝑑ℤsubscriptsubscript𝐴𝑑subscript𝒜𝑖subscript𝐴𝑑R_{i}:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{d\in D_{i}}A_{d}=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{A_{d}\in\mathcal{A}_{i}}A_{d},

be the set of elements not contained in any of the progressions of 𝒜isubscript𝒜𝑖\mathcal{A}_{i}, so that R0=ℤsubscript𝑅0ℤR_{0}=\mathbb{Z} and Rn=Rsubscript𝑅𝑛𝑅R_{n}=R. We also write Ni:=Di∖Di−1assignsubscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝐷𝑖1N_{i}:=D_{i}\setminus D_{i-1} for the set of “new” differences at the i𝑖ith stage, and define

Bi:=⋃d∈NiAdassignsubscript𝐵𝑖subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝐴𝑑B_{i}:=\bigcup_{d\in N_{i}}A_{d} (3)

to be the union of the arithmetic progressions exposed at step i𝑖i, so that Ri=Ri−1∖Bisubscript𝑅𝑖subscript𝑅𝑖1subscript𝐵𝑖R_{i}=R_{i-1}\setminus B_{i}.

It will be convenient to consider Risubscript𝑅𝑖R_{i} as a subset of the cyclic group ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}} (or of ℤQsubscriptℤ𝑄\mathbb{Z}_{Q}), which is possible because for each d∈Di𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖d\in D_{i} the set Adsubscript𝐴𝑑A_{d} is periodic with period d∣Qiconditional𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖d\mid Q_{i}. In particular, note that the density of Risubscript𝑅𝑖R_{i} in ℤℤ\mathbb{Z} is equal to the measure of the set Risubscript𝑅𝑖R_{i} in the uniform probability measure on the finite set ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}}. During the proof we will in fact need to consider non-uniform probability measures ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} on ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}}; note that each such measure can be extended (uniformly on each congruence class mod Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Q_{i}) to a probability measure on ℤQsubscriptℤ𝑄\mathbb{Z}_{Q}.222To be precise, we can set Q⋅ℙi⁡(x+Q​ℤ):=Qi⋅ℙi⁡(x+Qi​ℤ)assign⋅𝑄subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥𝑄ℤ⋅subscript𝑄𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥subscript𝑄𝑖ℤQ\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x+Q\mathbb{Z}):=Q_{i}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x+Q_{i}\mathbb{Z}). Note that, since gcd⁡(Qi,Q/Qi)=1subscript𝑄𝑖𝑄subscript𝑄𝑖1\gcd(Q_{i},Q/Q_{i})=1, we can (via the Chinese Remainder Theorem) consider ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} on ℤQ≅ℤQi×ℤQ/Qisubscriptℤ𝑄subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖subscriptℤ𝑄subscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q}\cong\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}}\times\mathbb{Z}_{Q/Q_{i}} as a product measure of ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} on ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}} with the uniform measure on ℤQ/Qisubscriptℤ𝑄subscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q/Q_{i}}. We will borrow (and abuse) terminology from measure theory by calling a subset S⊆ℤQ𝑆subscriptℤ𝑄S\subseteq\mathbb{Z}_{Q} (or a Q𝑄Q-periodic set S⊆ℤ𝑆ℤS\subseteq\mathbb{Z}) Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Q_{i}-measurable if S𝑆S is a union of congruence classes mod Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Q_{i}.

2.1. A sketch of the method

The basic idea is quite simple. We construct measures ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} in such a way that ℙi⁡(Bi)subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i}) is small, but without changing the measure of Bjsubscript𝐵𝑗B_{j} for any j<i𝑗𝑖j<i. It follows that the measure of ℤ∖Rℤ𝑅\mathbb{Z}\setminus R in the final measure ℙnsubscriptℙ𝑛\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n} is at most ∑iℙi⁡(Bi)subscript𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖\sum_{i}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i}), and thus if this quantity is less than 1, it follows that the arithmetic progressions do not cover ℤℤ\mathbb{Z}.

To bound ℙi⁡(Bi)subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i}), we use the 1st and 2nd moment methods (see Lemma 3.3, below). More precisely, we bound the expectation (in the measure ℙi−1subscriptℙ𝑖1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}) of the proportion of the ‘fibre’ F​(x)={(x,y):y∈ℤpiγi}𝐹𝑥conditional-set𝑥𝑦𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖F(x)=\big{\{}(x,y):y\in\mathbb{Z}_{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}}\big{\}} of x∈ℤQi−1𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}} removed in step i𝑖i, and the expectation of the square of this quantity. Bounding these moments is not too difficult, see Lemmas 3.6 and 3.7, below.

Finally, to deduce a lower bound on the uncovered set in the uniform measure, ℙ0subscriptℙ0\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}, we shall need to bound the average distortion ℙn⁡(x)/ℙ0⁡(x)subscriptℙ𝑛𝑥subscriptℙ0𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(x)/\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(x). We will design the measures ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} so that the ‘average’ here (which we take in the ℙnsubscriptℙ𝑛\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}-measure) is enough (by a convexity argument) to give such a lower bound, see Lemma 3.5.

2.2. The probability measures ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}

We will next define the non-uniform probability measures ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}, which are inspired by (but different in important ways to) a sequence of measures used in [8], and which will play a key role in the proof of Theorem 1.1. The rough idea is that we would like to distort the space so as to ‘blow up’ the uncovered set, but without increasing the measure of any single point too much. More precisely, we will define the measures inductively, choosing ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} so that it agrees with ℙi−1subscriptℙ𝑖1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1} on Qi−1subscript𝑄𝑖1Q_{i-1}-measurable sets, is not too much larger that ℙi−1subscriptℙ𝑖1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1} anywhere, and (subject to these conditons) is as small as possible on the set Bisubscript𝐵𝑖B_{i}, the union of the arithmetic progressions removed at step i𝑖i.

First, let ℙ0subscriptℙ0\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0} be the trivial probability measure on ℤQ0=ℤ1subscriptℤsubscript𝑄0subscriptℤ1\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{0}}=\mathbb{Z}_{1}, or (equivalently, by the comments above) the uniform measure on ℤQsubscriptℤ𝑄\mathbb{Z}_{Q}. Now fix δ1,…,δn∈[0,1/2]subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛012\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}\in[0,1/2], let i∈[n]𝑖delimited-[]𝑛i\in[n], and suppose we have already defined a probability measure ℙi−1subscriptℙ𝑖1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1} on ℤQi−1subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}. Our task is to define a probability measure ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} on ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}}.

In order to view Ri−1subscript𝑅𝑖1R_{i-1} and Risubscript𝑅𝑖R_{i} as subsets of the same set, let us (by the Chinese Remainder Theorem) represent ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}} as ℤQi−1×ℤpiγisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}\times\mathbb{Z}_{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}}, and denote elements of ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}} by pairs (x,y)𝑥𝑦(x,y), where x∈ℤQi−1𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}} and y∈ℤpiγi𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖y\in\mathbb{Z}_{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}}. We may view Ri−1subscript𝑅𝑖1R_{i-1} as a collection of ‘fibres’ of the form F​(x)={(x,y):y∈ℤpiγi}𝐹𝑥conditional-set𝑥𝑦𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖F(x)=\{(x,y):y\in\mathbb{Z}_{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}}\}, noting that ℙi−1subscriptℙ𝑖1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1} is uniform on each fibre, and view Risubscript𝑅𝑖R_{i} as being obtained from Ri−1subscript𝑅𝑖1R_{i-1} by removing the points that are contained in the new progressions of 𝒜i∖𝒜i−1subscript𝒜𝑖subscript𝒜𝑖1\mathcal{A}_{i}\setminus\mathcal{A}_{i-1}.

Now, for each x∈ℤQi−1𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}, define

αi​(x)=ℙi−1⁡(F​(x)∩Bi)ℙi−1⁡(x)=|{y∈ℤpiγi:(x,y)∈Bi}|piγi,subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscriptℙ𝑖1𝐹𝑥subscript𝐵𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥conditional-set𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖𝑥𝑦subscript𝐵𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖\alpha_{i}(x)=\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}\big{(}F(x)\cap B_{i}\big{)}}{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x)}=\frac{\big{|}\big{\{}y\in\mathbb{Z}_{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}}:(x,y)\in B_{i}\big{\}}\big{|}}{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}}, (4)

that is, the proportion of the fibre F​(x)𝐹𝑥F(x) that is removed at stage i𝑖i. The probability measure ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} on ℤQisubscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}} is defined as follows:

ℙi⁡(x,y):={max⁡{0,αi​(x)−δiαi​(x)​(1−δi)}⋅ℙi−1⁡(x,y),if ​(x,y)∈Bi;min⁡{11−αi​(x),11−δi}⋅ℙi−1⁡(x,y),if ​(x,y)∉Bi.assignsubscriptℙ𝑖𝑥𝑦cases⋅0subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥1subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥𝑦if 𝑥𝑦subscript𝐵𝑖⋅11subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥11subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥𝑦if 𝑥𝑦subscript𝐵𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x,y):=\begin{cases}\max\bigg{\{}0,\,\displaystyle\frac{\alpha_{i}(x)-\delta_{i}}{\alpha_{i}(x)(1-\delta_{i})}\bigg{\}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x,y),&\text{if }(x,y)\in B_{i};\\[8.61108pt] \min\bigg{\{}\displaystyle\frac{1}{1-\alpha_{i}(x)},\,\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\bigg{\}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x,y),&\text{if }(x,y)\notin B_{i}.\end{cases} (5)

This is an important (and slightly technical) definition, and therefore deserves some additional explanation. First, observe that if αi​(x)⩽δisubscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖\alpha_{i}(x)\leqslant\delta_{i}, then ℙi⁡(x,y)=0subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥𝑦0\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x,y)=0 for every element of Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Q_{i} that is covered in step i𝑖i, and that the measure is increased proportionally elsewhere to compensate. On the other hand, for those x∈ℤQi−1𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}} for which αi​(x)>δisubscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖\alpha_{i}(x)>\delta_{i}, we ‘cap’ the distortion by increasing the measure at each point not covered in step i𝑖i by a factor of 1/(1−δi)11subscript𝛿𝑖1/(1-\delta_{i}), and decreasing the measure on removed points by a corresponding factor.

The following simple properties of the measure ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} will be useful in the proof.

Lemma 2.1.

ℙi⁡(S)=ℙi−1⁡(S)subscriptℙ𝑖𝑆subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑆\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(S)=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(S) for any Qi−1subscript𝑄𝑖1Q_{i-1}-measurable set S𝑆S.

Proof.

Let x∈ℤQi−1𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}, and observe first that if αi​(x)⩽δisubscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖\alpha_{i}(x)\leqslant\delta_{i}, then

ℙi⁡(x)=(αi​(x)⋅0+(1−αi​(x))⋅11−αi​(x))⋅ℙi−1⁡(x)=ℙi−1⁡(x),subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥⋅⋅subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥0⋅1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥11subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x)=\Big{(}\alpha_{i}(x)\cdot 0+\big{(}1-\alpha_{i}(x)\big{)}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\alpha_{i}(x)}\Big{)}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x)=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x),

where ℙi⁡(x)=∑(x,y)∈F​(x)ℙi⁡(x,y)subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥subscript𝑥𝑦𝐹𝑥subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥𝑦\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x)=\sum_{(x,y)\in F(x)}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x,y). On the other hand, if αi​(x)>δisubscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖\alpha_{i}(x)>\delta_{i} then

ℙi⁡(x)=(αi​(x)⋅αi​(x)−δiαi​(x)​(1−δi)+(1−αi​(x))⋅11−δi)⋅ℙi−1⁡(x)=ℙi−1⁡(x).subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥⋅⋅subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥1subscript𝛿𝑖⋅1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥11subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x)=\Big{(}\alpha_{i}(x)\cdot\frac{\alpha_{i}(x)-\delta_{i}}{\alpha_{i}(x)(1-\delta_{i})}+\big{(}1-\alpha_{i}(x)\big{)}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\Big{)}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x)=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x).

Summing over x∈S𝑥𝑆x\in S, we obtain ℙi⁡(S)=ℙi−1⁡(S)subscriptℙ𝑖𝑆subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑆\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(S)=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(S), as claimed. ∎

Lemma 2.2.

For any set S⊆ℤQ𝑆subscriptℤ𝑄S\subseteq\mathbb{Z}_{Q}, we have

ℙi⁡(S)⩽11−δi⋅ℙi−1⁡(S).subscriptℙ𝑖𝑆⋅11subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑆\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(S)\leqslant\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(S). (6)

Moreover, if S⊆Bi𝑆subscript𝐵𝑖S\subseteq B_{i} then

ℙi⁡(S)⩽ℙi−1⁡(S).subscriptℙ𝑖𝑆subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑆\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(S)\leqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(S). (7)
Proof.

Both inequalities follow immediately (for each ‘atom’ S={(x,y)}𝑆𝑥𝑦S=\{(x,y)\}, and hence also in general, by additivity) from (5). ∎

Given a function f:ℤQ→ℝ⩾0:𝑓→subscriptℤ𝑄subscriptℝabsent0f\colon\mathbb{Z}_{Q}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}_{\geqslant 0}, let us define the expectation of f𝑓f with respect to ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} to be

𝔼i⁡[f​(x)]:=∑x∈ℤQf​(x)​ℙi⁡(x).assignsubscript𝔼𝑖𝑓𝑥subscript𝑥subscriptℤ𝑄𝑓𝑥subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}f(x)\big{]}:=\sum_{x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q}}f(x)\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x).

By the observations above, we have

𝔼i⁡[f​(x)]⩽11−δi⋅𝔼i−1⁡[f​(x)],subscript𝔼𝑖𝑓𝑥⋅11subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝔼𝑖1𝑓𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}f(x)\big{]}\leqslant\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}f(x)\big{]},

and moreover 𝔼i⁡[f​(x)]⩽𝔼i−1⁡[f​(x)]subscript𝔼𝑖𝑓𝑥subscript𝔼𝑖1𝑓𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}[f(x)]\leqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}[f(x)] if f𝑓f is supported on Bisubscript𝐵𝑖B_{i}, and 𝔼i⁡[f​(x)]=𝔼i−1⁡[f​(x)]subscript𝔼𝑖𝑓𝑥subscript𝔼𝑖1𝑓𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}[f(x)]=\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}[f(x)] if f𝑓f is Qi−1subscript𝑄𝑖1Q_{i-1}-measurable.

3. A general theorem

In this section we will prove our main technical results, Theorems 3.1 and 3.2, below, which together imply Theorems 1.1, 1.2 and 1.4, and also Hough’s theorem. In each case the deduction involves little more than choosing a suitable sequence (δ1,…,δn)subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛(\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}).

Given a finite collection 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} of arithmetic progressions, let n𝑛n be the number of distinct prime factors of Q=lcm⁡(D)𝑄lcm𝐷Q=\operatorname{lcm}(D), and for each sequence δ1,…,δn∈[0,1/2]subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛012\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}\in[0,1/2], let the probability distributions ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} and functions αi:ℤQi−1→[0,1]:subscript𝛼𝑖→subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖101\alpha_{i}\colon\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}\rightarrow[0,1] be as defined in (4) and (5). Set

Mi(1):=𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)]andMi(2):=𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)2],formulae-sequenceassignsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥andassignsuperscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥2M_{i}^{(1)}:=\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)\big{]}\qquad\text{and}\qquad M_{i}^{(2)}:=\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{2}\big{]},

and define a multiplicative function ν𝜈\nu, defined on factors of Q𝑄Q, by setting

ν​(d)=∏pj∣d11−δj.𝜈𝑑subscriptproductconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑑11subscript𝛿𝑗\nu(d)=\prod_{p_{j}\mid d}\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}}. (8)

for each d∣Qconditional𝑑𝑄d\mid Q.

Theorem 3.1.

Let 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} be a finite collection of arithmetic progressions, and let δ1,…,δn∈[0,1/2]subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛012\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}\in[0,1/2]. If

η:=∑i=1nmin⁡{Mi(1),Mi(2)4​δi​(1−δi)}<1,assign𝜂superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖24subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖1\eta:=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\min\bigg{\{}M_{i}^{(1)},\frac{M_{i}^{(2)}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}\bigg{\}}<1, (9)

then 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers. Moreover, the uncovered set R𝑅R has density at least

ℙ0⁡(R)⩾(1−η)​exp⁡(−21−η​∑d∈Dν​(d)d).subscriptℙ0𝑅1𝜂21𝜂subscript𝑑𝐷𝜈𝑑𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R)\geqslant\big{(}1-\eta\big{)}\exp\bigg{(}-\frac{2}{1-\eta}\sum_{d\in D}\frac{\nu(d)}{d}\bigg{)}. (10)

In order to show that (9) holds in our applications, we need to bound the moments of αi​(x)subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥\alpha_{i}(x). The following technical theorem provides general bounds that are sufficient in most cases.

Theorem 3.2.

Let 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} be a finite collection of arithmetic progressions, and let δ1,…,δn∈[0,1/2]subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛012\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}\in[0,1/2]. Then

Mi(1)⩽∑m​pij∈Ni,m∣Qi−1pi−j⋅ν​(m)m⩽1pi−1​∏j<i(1+1(1−δj)​(pj−1)),superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1subscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗subscript𝑁𝑖conditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝜈𝑚𝑚1subscript𝑝𝑖1subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖111subscript𝛿𝑗subscript𝑝𝑗1M_{i}^{(1)}\leqslant\sum_{mp_{i}^{j}\in N_{i},\,m\mid Q_{i-1}}p_{i}^{-j}\cdot\frac{\nu(m)}{m}\leqslant\frac{1}{p_{i}-1}\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)}\bigg{)},

and

Mi(2)⩽∑m1​pij1,m2​pij2∈Nim1,m2∣Qi−1pi−(j1+j2)⋅ν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)⩽1(pi−1)2​∏j<i(1+3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2).superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2subscriptsubscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑚2superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗2subscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝑚1conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑄𝑖1⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖13subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m_{1}p_{i}^{j_{1}},\,m_{2}p_{i}^{j_{2}}\in N_{i}\\ m_{1},m_{2}\,\mid\,Q_{i-1}\end{subarray}}p_{i}^{-(j_{1}+j_{2})}\cdot\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}.

The proofs of Theorems 3.1 and 3.2 are both surprisingly simple. Let us begin with the following easy lemma, which is the first step in the proof of Theorem 3.1. We assume throughout this section that 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} is a given finite collection of arithmetic progressions such that Q=lcm⁡(D)𝑄lcm𝐷Q=\operatorname{lcm}(D) has exactly n𝑛n distinct prime factors, and fix a sequence δ1,…,δn∈[0,1/2]subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛012\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}\in[0,1/2], and hence a function αisubscript𝛼𝑖\alpha_{i} and measure ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} for each i∈[n]𝑖delimited-[]𝑛i\in[n].

Lemma 3.3.
ℙi⁡(Bi)⩽min⁡{𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)],𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)2]4​δi​(1−δi)}.subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥24subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i})\leqslant\min\bigg{\{}\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)\big{]},\ \frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{2}\big{]}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}\bigg{\}}.
Proof.

Observe first that

ℙi⁡(Bi)⩽ℙi−1⁡(Bi)=𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)],subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1subscript𝐵𝑖subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i})\leqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(B_{i})=\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)\big{]},

where the inequality holds by (7), and the equality by (4).

For the other upper bound, we will use the elementary inequality max⁡{a−d,0}⩽a2/4​d𝑎𝑑0superscript𝑎24𝑑\max\{a-d,0\}\leqslant a^{2}/4d, which is easily seen to hold for all a,d>0𝑎𝑑0a,d>0 by rearranging the inequality (a−2​d)2⩾0superscript𝑎2𝑑20(a-2d)^{2}\geqslant 0. By (4) and (5) (the definitions of αisubscript𝛼𝑖\alpha_{i} and ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}), we have

ℙi⁡(Bi)subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖\displaystyle\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i}) =∑x∈ℤQi−1max⁡{0,αi​(x)−δiαi​(x)​(1−δi)}⋅ℙi−1⁡(F​(x)∩Bi)absentsubscript𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1⋅0subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥1subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝐹𝑥subscript𝐵𝑖\displaystyle=\sum_{x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}}\max\bigg{\{}0,\,\frac{\alpha_{i}(x)-\delta_{i}}{\alpha_{i}(x)(1-\delta_{i})}\bigg{\}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}\big{(}F(x)\cap B_{i}\big{)}
=11−δi​∑x∈ℤQi−1max⁡{0,αi​(x)−δi}⋅ℙi−1⁡(x)absent11subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1⋅0subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥\displaystyle=\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\sum_{x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}}\max\big{\{}0,\,\alpha_{i}(x)-\delta_{i}\big{\}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x)
⩽11−δi​∑x∈ℤQi−1αi​(x)24​δi⋅ℙi−1⁡(x)=𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)2]4​δi​(1−δi),absent11subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1⋅subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥24subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥24subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖\displaystyle\leqslant\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\sum_{x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}}}\frac{\alpha_{i}(x)^{2}}{4\delta_{i}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}(x)=\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{2}\big{]}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})},

as required. ∎

It is already straightforward to deduce from Lemma 3.3 and inequality (9) that 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers. In order to deduce the bound (10) on the density of the uncovered set, we will need to work slightly harder. First, we need the following easy bound on the ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}-measure of an arithmetic progression.

Lemma 3.4.

For each 0⩽i⩽n0𝑖𝑛0\leqslant i\leqslant n, and all b,d∈ℤ𝑏𝑑ℤb,d\in\mathbb{Z} such that d∣Qconditional𝑑𝑄d\mid Q, we have

ℙi⁡(b+d​ℤ)⩽1d​∏pj∣d,j⩽i11−δj=ν​(gcd⁡(d,Qi))d.subscriptℙ𝑖𝑏𝑑ℤ1𝑑subscriptproductconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑑𝑗𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑗𝜈𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}\big{(}b+d\mathbb{Z}\big{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{d}\prod_{p_{j}\mid d,\,j\leqslant i}\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}}=\frac{\nu\big{(}\gcd(d,Q_{i})\big{)}}{d}. (11)
Proof.

The proof is by induction on i𝑖i. Note first that ℙ0subscriptℙ0\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0} is just the uniform measure, so ℙ0⁡(b+d​ℤ)=1/dsubscriptℙ0𝑏𝑑ℤ1𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(b+d\mathbb{Z})=1/d. So let i∈[n]𝑖delimited-[]𝑛i\in[n], and assume that the claimed bound holds for ℙi−1subscriptℙ𝑖1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}. Suppose first that pi∣dconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑑p_{i}\mid d. Then, by (6) and the induction hypothesis, we have

ℙi⁡(b+d​ℤ)⩽11−δi⋅ℙi−1⁡(b+d​ℤ)⩽1d⋅11−δi​∏pj∣d,j<i11−δj=1d​∏pj∣d,j⩽i11−δj,subscriptℙ𝑖𝑏𝑑ℤ⋅11subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑏𝑑ℤ⋅1𝑑11subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptproductconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑑𝑗𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑗1𝑑subscriptproductconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑑𝑗𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑗\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}\big{(}b+d\mathbb{Z}\big{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}\big{(}b+d\mathbb{Z}\big{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{d}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\prod_{p_{j}\mid d,\,j<i}\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}}=\frac{1}{d}\prod_{p_{j}\mid d,\,j\leqslant i}\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}},

as required. On the other hand, if pi∤dnot-dividessubscript𝑝𝑖𝑑p_{i}\nmid d then we may write d=m​ℓ𝑑𝑚ℓd=m\ell, where m=gcd⁡(d,Qi)=gcd⁡(d,Qi−1)𝑚𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖1m=\gcd(d,Q_{i})=\gcd(d,Q_{i-1}). It follows that

ℙi⁡(b+d​ℤ)=ℙi⁡(b+m​ℤ)ℓ=ℙi−1⁡(b+m​ℤ)ℓ⩽1ℓ​m​∏pj∣d,j<i11−δj=1d​∏pj∣d,j⩽i11−δj,subscriptℙ𝑖𝑏𝑑ℤsubscriptℙ𝑖𝑏𝑚ℤℓsubscriptℙ𝑖1𝑏𝑚ℤℓ1ℓ𝑚subscriptproductconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑑𝑗𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑗1𝑑subscriptproductconditionalsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑑𝑗𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑗\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}\big{(}b+d\mathbb{Z}\big{)}=\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}\big{(}b+m\mathbb{Z}\big{)}}{\ell}=\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}\big{(}b+m\mathbb{Z}\big{)}}{\ell}\leqslant\frac{1}{\ell m}\prod_{p_{j}\mid d,\,j<i}\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}}=\frac{1}{d}\prod_{p_{j}\mid d,\,j\leqslant i}\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}},

as required. ∎

Let us define the distortion Δi​(x)subscriptΔ𝑖𝑥\Delta_{i}(x) of a point x∈ℤQi𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}} by

Δi​(x):=max⁡{0,log⁡ℙi⁡(x)ℙ0⁡(x)}.assignsubscriptΔ𝑖𝑥0subscriptℙ𝑖𝑥subscriptℙ0𝑥\Delta_{i}(x):=\max\bigg{\{}0,\,\log\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(x)}{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(x)}\bigg{\}}. (12)

The following bound on the average distortion will allow us to prove (10).

Lemma 3.5.

For each 0⩽i⩽n0𝑖𝑛0\leqslant i\leqslant n, we have

𝔼i⁡[Δi​(x)]⩽2⋅∑d∈Diν​(d)d.subscript𝔼𝑖subscriptΔ𝑖𝑥⋅2subscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖𝜈𝑑𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}\Delta_{i}(x)\big{]}\leqslant 2\cdot\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{\nu(d)}{d}.
Proof.

We claim first that

log⁡ℙj⁡(x)ℙj−1⁡(x)⩽2⋅αj​(x)subscriptℙ𝑗𝑥subscriptℙ𝑗1𝑥⋅2subscript𝛼𝑗𝑥\log\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j}(x)}{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j-1}(x)}\leqslant 2\cdot\alpha_{j}(x)

for every j∈[n]𝑗delimited-[]𝑛j\in[n] and x∈ℤQj𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑗x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{j}}. Indeed, observe that ℙj−1⁡(x)/ℙj⁡(x)⩾max⁡{1−αj​(x),1−δj}subscriptℙ𝑗1𝑥subscriptℙ𝑗𝑥1subscript𝛼𝑗𝑥1subscript𝛿𝑗\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j-1}(x)/\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j}(x)\geqslant\max\{1-\alpha_{j}(x),1-\delta_{j}\}, by (5), and use the inequality −log⁡(1−z)⩽2​z1𝑧2𝑧-\log(1-z)\leqslant 2z, which holds for z⩽1/2𝑧12z\leqslant 1/2, and the fact that δj⩽1/2subscript𝛿𝑗12\delta_{j}\leqslant 1/2. It follows that

𝔼i⁡[Δi​(x)]⩽∑j=1i𝔼i⁡[max⁡{0,log⁡ℙj⁡(x)ℙj−1⁡(x)}]⩽2⋅∑j=1i𝔼i⁡[αj​(x)].subscript𝔼𝑖subscriptΔ𝑖𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑖subscript𝔼𝑖0subscriptℙ𝑗𝑥subscriptℙ𝑗1𝑥⋅2superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑖subscript𝔼𝑖subscript𝛼𝑗𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}\Delta_{i}(x)\big{]}\leqslant\sum_{j=1}^{i}\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\bigg{[}\max\bigg{\{}0,\,\log\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j}(x)}{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j-1}(x)}\bigg{\}}\bigg{]}\leqslant 2\cdot\sum_{j=1}^{i}\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}\alpha_{j}(x)\big{]}.

Now, by (4) and Lemma 2.1, we have

𝔼i⁡[αj​(x)]=𝔼j−1⁡[αj​(x)]=ℙj−1⁡(Bj)subscript𝔼𝑖subscript𝛼𝑗𝑥subscript𝔼𝑗1subscript𝛼𝑗𝑥subscriptℙ𝑗1subscript𝐵𝑗\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}\alpha_{j}(x)\big{]}=\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{j-1}\big{[}\alpha_{j}(x)\big{]}=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j-1}(B_{j})

for each j∈[i]𝑗delimited-[]𝑖j\in[i], since the function αjsubscript𝛼𝑗\alpha_{j} is Qj−1subscript𝑄𝑗1Q_{j-1}-measurable. Moreover, by (3) (the definition of Bisubscript𝐵𝑖B_{i}), the union bound, and Lemma 3.4, we have

ℙj−1⁡(Bj)⩽∑d∈Njℙj−1⁡(Ad)⩽∑d∈Njν​(gcd⁡(d,Qj−1))d⩽∑d∈Njν​(d)d.subscriptℙ𝑗1subscript𝐵𝑗subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑗subscriptℙ𝑗1subscript𝐴𝑑subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑗𝜈𝑑subscript𝑄𝑗1𝑑subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑗𝜈𝑑𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j-1}(B_{j})\leqslant\sum_{d\in N_{j}}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{j-1}(A_{d})\leqslant\sum_{d\in N_{j}}\frac{\nu(\gcd(d,Q_{j-1}))}{d}\leqslant\sum_{d\in N_{j}}\frac{\nu(d)}{d}.

Hence we obtain

𝔼i⁡[Δi​(x)]⩽2⋅∑j=1i∑d∈Njν​(d)d=2⋅∑d∈Diν​(d)d,subscript𝔼𝑖subscriptΔ𝑖𝑥⋅2superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑖subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑗𝜈𝑑𝑑⋅2subscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖𝜈𝑑𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i}\big{[}\Delta_{i}(x)\big{]}\leqslant 2\cdot\sum_{j=1}^{i}\sum_{d\in N_{j}}\frac{\nu(d)}{d}=2\cdot\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{\nu(d)}{d},

as claimed. ∎

Theorem 3.1 now follows easily from Lemmas 3.3 and 3.5.

Proof of Theorem 3.1.

We claim first that

1−ℙn⁡(R)⩽∑i=1nℙn⁡(Bi)=∑i=1nℙi⁡(Bi)⩽η.1subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛subscriptℙ𝑛subscript𝐵𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖𝜂1-\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\leqslant\sum_{i=1}^{n}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(B_{i})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i})\leqslant\eta. (13)

Indeed, the first inequality is just the union bound; the equality holds by Lemma 2.1, since Bisubscript𝐵𝑖B_{i} is Qisubscript𝑄𝑖Q_{i}-measurable, so ℙi⁡(Bi)=ℙi+1⁡(Bi)=⋯=ℙn⁡(Bi)subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖1subscript𝐵𝑖⋯subscriptℙ𝑛subscript𝐵𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i})=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i+1}(B_{i})=\dots=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(B_{i}); and the final inequality follows from Lemma 3.3 and (9), the definition of η𝜂\eta. It follows that ℙn⁡(R)⩾1−η>0subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅1𝜂0\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\geqslant 1-\eta>0 if η<1𝜂1\eta<1, and hence R𝑅R is non-empty, i.e., 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers.

To prove the claimed lower bound on the density of the uncovered set, we will use Lemma 3.5. Indeed, by the definition (12) of Δn​(x)subscriptΔ𝑛𝑥\Delta_{n}(x), we have

ℙ0⁡(R)=𝔼0⁡[1​1x∈R]⩾𝔼n⁡[1​1x∈R​exp⁡(−Δn​(x))].subscriptℙ0𝑅subscript𝔼0subscript11𝑥𝑅subscript𝔼𝑛subscript11𝑥𝑅subscriptΔ𝑛𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R)=\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{0}\big{[}\hbox{$1\mkern-6.5mu1$}_{x\in R}\big{]}\geqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\hbox{$1\mkern-6.5mu1$}_{x\in R}\exp\big{(}-\Delta_{n}(x)\big{)}\big{]}.

Now, by the convexity of e−zsuperscript𝑒𝑧e^{-z}, and noting that ℙn⁡(R)⋅𝔼n⁡[Δn​(x)∣x∈R]⩽𝔼n⁡[Δn​(x)]⋅subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅subscript𝔼𝑛conditionalsubscriptΔ𝑛𝑥𝑥𝑅subscript𝔼𝑛subscriptΔ𝑛𝑥\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\Delta_{n}(x)\mid x\in R\big{]}\leqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\Delta_{n}(x)\big{]},

𝔼n⁡[1​1x∈R​exp⁡(−Δn​(x))]subscript𝔼𝑛subscript11𝑥𝑅subscriptΔ𝑛𝑥\displaystyle\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\hbox{$1\mkern-6.5mu1$}_{x\in R}\exp\big{(}-\Delta_{n}(x)\big{)}\big{]} =ℙn⁡(R)⋅𝔼n⁡[exp⁡(−Δn​(x))|x∈R]absent⋅subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅subscript𝔼𝑛|subscriptΔ𝑛𝑥𝑥𝑅\displaystyle=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\exp\big{(}-\Delta_{n}(x)\big{)}\mathrel{\big{|}}x\in R\big{]}
⩾ℙn⁡(R)⋅exp⁡(−𝔼n⁡[Δn​(x)∣x∈R])absent⋅subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅subscript𝔼𝑛conditionalsubscriptΔ𝑛𝑥𝑥𝑅\displaystyle\geqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\cdot\exp\big{(}-\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\Delta_{n}(x)\mid x\in R\big{]}\big{)}
⩾ℙn⁡(R)⋅exp⁡(−𝔼n⁡[Δn​(x)]ℙn⁡(R)).absent⋅subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅subscript𝔼𝑛subscriptΔ𝑛𝑥subscriptℙ𝑛𝑅\displaystyle\geqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\cdot\exp\bigg{(}-\frac{\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{n}\big{[}\Delta_{n}(x)\big{]}}{\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)}\bigg{)}.

Hence, by Lemma 3.5, and since ℙn⁡(R)⩾1−ηsubscriptℙ𝑛𝑅1𝜂\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{n}(R)\geqslant 1-\eta, by (13), we obtain

ℙ0⁡(R)⩾(1−η)​exp⁡(−21−η​∑d∈Dν​(d)d),subscriptℙ0𝑅1𝜂21𝜂subscript𝑑𝐷𝜈𝑑𝑑\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R)\geqslant\big{(}1-\eta\big{)}\exp\bigg{(}-\frac{2}{1-\eta}\sum_{d\in D}\frac{\nu(d)}{d}\bigg{)},

as required. ∎

3.1. Bounding the moments of αi​(x)subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥\alpha_{i}(x)

The proof of Theorem 3.2 is also quite straightforward. First, recall that Ni:=Di∖Di−1assignsubscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝐷𝑖1N_{i}:=D_{i}\setminus D_{i-1} is the set of new differences at step i𝑖i, and note that any d∈Ni𝑑subscript𝑁𝑖d\in N_{i} can be represented in the form d=m​pij𝑑𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗d=mp_{i}^{j}, where m∣Qi−1conditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1m\mid Q_{i-1} and 1⩽j⩽γi1𝑗subscript𝛾𝑖1\leqslant j\leqslant\gamma_{i}. The first step is the following general bound on the moments of αi​(x)subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥\alpha_{i}(x).

Lemma 3.6.

For each k∈ℕ𝑘ℕk\in\mathbb{N},

𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)k]subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥𝑘\displaystyle\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{k}\big{]} ⩽∑m1​pij1,…,mk​pijk∈Nim1,…,mk∣Qi−11pij1+⋯+jk⋅ν​(lcm⁡(m1,…,mk))lcm⁡(m1,…,mk)absentsubscriptsubscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1…subscript𝑚𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗𝑘subscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝑚1…conditionalsubscript𝑚𝑘subscript𝑄𝑖1⋅1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1⋯subscript𝑗𝑘𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘\displaystyle\leqslant\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m_{1}p_{i}^{j_{1}},\dots,m_{k}p_{i}^{j_{k}}\in N_{i}\\ m_{1},\dots,m_{k}\,\mid\,Q_{i-1}\end{subarray}}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+\dots+j_{k}}}\cdot\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})} (14)
⩽1(pi−1)k​∑m1,…,mk∣Qi−1ν​(lcm⁡(m1,…,mk))lcm⁡(m1,…,mk).absent1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖1𝑘subscriptsubscript𝑚1…conditionalsubscript𝑚𝑘subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘\displaystyle\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{k}}\sum_{m_{1},\dots,m_{k}\,\mid\,Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})}. (15)
Proof.

Recall the definitions (3) and (4) of the set Bisubscript𝐵𝑖B_{i} and the function αisubscript𝛼𝑖\alpha_{i}, respectively. Applying the union bound, we obtain, for each x∈ℤQi−1𝑥subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖1x\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i-1}},

αi​(x)=∑(x,y)∈ℤQipi−γi⋅1​1​[(x,y)∈Bi]⩽∑(x,y)∈ℤQi∑d∈Nipi−γi⋅1​1​[(x,y)∈Ad].subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝑥𝑦subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖11delimited-[]𝑥𝑦subscript𝐵𝑖subscript𝑥𝑦subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑖⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖11delimited-[]𝑥𝑦subscript𝐴𝑑\alpha_{i}(x)\,=\sum_{(x,y)\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}}}p_{i}^{-\gamma_{i}}\cdot\hbox{$1\mkern-6.5mu1$}\big{[}(x,y)\in B_{i}\big{]}\leqslant\sum_{(x,y)\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}}}\sum_{d\in N_{i}}p_{i}^{-\gamma_{i}}\cdot\hbox{$1\mkern-6.5mu1$}\big{[}(x,y)\in A_{d}\big{]}.

Now, observe that if d=m​pij∈Ni𝑑𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗subscript𝑁𝑖d=mp_{i}^{j}\in N_{i}, where pi∤mnot-dividessubscript𝑝𝑖𝑚p_{i}\nmid m, then there are either zero or pγi−jsuperscript𝑝subscript𝛾𝑖𝑗p^{\gamma_{i}-j} values of y∈ℤpiγi𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝛾𝑖y\in\mathbb{Z}_{p_{i}^{\gamma_{i}}} with (x,y)∈Ad𝑥𝑦subscript𝐴𝑑(x,y)\in A_{d}. Indeed, (x,y)∈Ad=ad+d​ℤ𝑥𝑦subscript𝐴𝑑subscript𝑎𝑑𝑑ℤ(x,y)\in A_{d}=a_{d}+d\mathbb{Z} iff x≡admodm𝑥modulosubscript𝑎𝑑𝑚x\equiv a_{d}\bmod m and y≡admodpij𝑦modulosubscript𝑎𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗y\equiv a_{d}\bmod p_{i}^{j}. It follows that

αi​(x)⩽∑d=m​pij∈Nipi−j⋅1​1​[x≡admodm],subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝑑𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗subscript𝑁𝑖⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗11delimited-[]𝑥modulosubscript𝑎𝑑𝑚\alpha_{i}(x)\leqslant\sum_{d=mp_{i}^{j}\in N_{i}}p_{i}^{-j}\cdot\hbox{$1\mkern-6.5mu1$}\big{[}x\equiv a_{d}\bmod m\big{]},

and hence

𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)k]⩽∑d1=m1​pij1∈Ni⋯​∑dk=mk​pijk∈Ni1pij1+⋯+jk​ℙi−1⁡(x≡adjmodmj​ for ​j∈[k]).subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥𝑘subscriptsubscript𝑑1subscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑁𝑖⋯subscriptsubscript𝑑𝑘subscript𝑚𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗𝑘subscript𝑁𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1⋯subscript𝑗𝑘subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥modulosubscript𝑎subscript𝑑𝑗subscript𝑚𝑗 for 𝑗delimited-[]𝑘\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{k}\big{]}\leqslant\sum_{d_{1}=m_{1}p_{i}^{j_{1}}\in N_{i}}\cdots\sum_{d_{k}=m_{k}p_{i}^{j_{k}}\in N_{i}}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+\dots+j_{k}}}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}\big{(}x\equiv a_{d_{j}}\bmod{m_{j}}\text{ for }j\in[k]\big{)}.

Now, note that the intersection of the events {x≡adjmodmj}𝑥modulosubscript𝑎subscript𝑑𝑗subscript𝑚𝑗\{x\equiv a_{d_{j}}\bmod m_{j}\} over j∈[k]𝑗delimited-[]𝑘j\in[k] is either empty (if the congruences are incompatible), or is equivalent to x≡bmodlcm⁡(m1,…,mk)𝑥modulo𝑏lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘x\equiv b\bmod\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k}) for some b𝑏b. Therefore, by Lemma 3.4, we have

ℙi−1⁡(x≡adjmodmj​ for ​j∈[k])⩽ν​(lcm⁡(m1,…,mk))lcm⁡(m1,…,mk),subscriptℙ𝑖1𝑥modulosubscript𝑎subscript𝑑𝑗subscript𝑚𝑗 for 𝑗delimited-[]𝑘𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i-1}\big{(}x\equiv a_{d_{j}}\bmod{m_{j}}\text{ for }j\in[k]\big{)}\leqslant\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})},

and hence

𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)k]subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥𝑘\displaystyle\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{k}\big{]} ⩽∑m1​pij1,…,mk​pijk∈Nim1,…,mk∣Qi−11pij1+⋯+jk⋅ν​(lcm⁡(m1,…,mk))lcm⁡(m1,…,mk)absentsubscriptsubscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1…subscript𝑚𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗𝑘subscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝑚1…conditionalsubscript𝑚𝑘subscript𝑄𝑖1⋅1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1⋯subscript𝑗𝑘𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘\displaystyle\leqslant\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m_{1}p_{i}^{j_{1}},\dots,m_{k}p_{i}^{j_{k}}\in N_{i}\\ m_{1},\dots,m_{k}\,\mid\,Q_{i-1}\end{subarray}}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+\dots+j_{k}}}\cdot\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})}
⩽∑j1,…,jk⩾11pij1+⋯+jk​∑m1,…,mk∣Qi−1ν​(lcm⁡(m1,…,mk))lcm⁡(m1,…,mk).absentsubscriptsubscript𝑗1…subscript𝑗𝑘11superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1⋯subscript𝑗𝑘subscriptsubscript𝑚1…conditionalsubscript𝑚𝑘subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘lcmsubscript𝑚1…subscript𝑚𝑘\displaystyle\leqslant\sum_{j_{1},\dots,j_{k}\geqslant 1}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+\dots+j_{k}}}\sum_{m_{1},\dots,m_{k}\,\mid\,Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},\dots,m_{k})}.

This proves (14); to obtain (15), simply note that

∑j1,…,jk⩾11pij1+⋯+jk=(∑j⩾11pij)k=1(pi−1)k.∎subscriptsubscript𝑗1…subscript𝑗𝑘11superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1⋯subscript𝑗𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑗11superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖1𝑘\sum_{j_{1},\dots,j_{k}\geqslant 1}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+\dots+j_{k}}}=\bigg{(}\sum_{j\geqslant 1}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j}}\bigg{)}^{k}=\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{k}}.\qed

To complete the proof of Theorem 3.2, it only remains to prove the following bounds.

Lemma 3.7.

For each i∈[n]𝑖delimited-[]𝑛i\in[n],

∑m∣Qi−1ν​(m)m⩽∏j<i(1+1(1−δj)​(pj−1))subscriptconditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈𝑚𝑚subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖111subscript𝛿𝑗subscript𝑝𝑗1\sum_{m\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu(m)}{m}\leqslant\,\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)}\bigg{)}

and

∑m1,m2∣Qi−1ν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)⩽∏j<i(1+3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2).subscriptsubscript𝑚1conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖13subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12\sum_{m_{1},m_{2}\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}\leqslant\,\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}.
Proof.

Recall from (8) that ν𝜈\nu is a multiplicative function. It follows that

∑m∣Qi−1ν​(m)m=∏j<i∑t=0γjν​(pjt)pjt,subscriptconditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈𝑚𝑚subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑡0subscript𝛾𝑗𝜈superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡\sum_{m\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu(m)}{m}=\,\prod_{j<i}\sum_{t=0}^{\gamma_{j}}\frac{\nu(p_{j}^{t})}{p_{j}^{t}},

and by (8), we have

∏j<i∑t=0γjν​(pjt)pjt=∏j<i(1+11−δj​∑t=1γj1pjt)⩽∏j<i(1+1(1−δj)​(pj−1)).subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑡0subscript𝛾𝑗𝜈superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖111subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑡1subscript𝛾𝑗1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖111subscript𝛿𝑗subscript𝑝𝑗1\prod_{j<i}\sum_{t=0}^{\gamma_{j}}\frac{\nu(p_{j}^{t})}{p_{j}^{t}}=\,\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{1-\delta_{j}}\sum_{t=1}^{\gamma_{j}}\frac{1}{p_{j}^{t}}\bigg{)}\leqslant\,\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)}\bigg{)}.

To prove the second inequality, let us write χ​(m)𝜒𝑚\chi(m) for the number of ways of representing a number m>1𝑚1m>1 as the least common multiple of two numbers, i.e.,

χ​(m):=|{(m1,m2):lcm⁡(m1,m2)=m}|,assign𝜒𝑚conditional-setsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2𝑚\chi(m):=\big{|}\big{\{}(m_{1},m_{2}):\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})=m\big{\}}\big{|},

so that

∑m1,m2∣Qi−1ν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)=∑m∣Qi−1χ​(m)​ν​(m)m.subscriptsubscript𝑚1conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscriptconditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜒𝑚𝜈𝑚𝑚\sum_{m_{1},m_{2}\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}=\sum_{m\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\chi(m)\nu(m)}{m}.

Observe that the function χ𝜒\chi is multiplicative and satisfies χ​(pt)=2​t+1𝜒superscript𝑝𝑡2𝑡1\chi(p^{t})=2t+1 for all primes p𝑝p and integers t⩾0𝑡0t\geqslant 0. It follows that

∑m∣Qi−1χ​(m)​ν​(m)m=∏j<i∑t=0γiχ​(pjt)​ν​(pjt)pjt⩽∏j<i(1+∑t=1∞2​t+1(1−δj)​pjt).subscriptconditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜒𝑚𝜈𝑚𝑚subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑡0subscript𝛾𝑖𝜒superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡𝜈superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑡12𝑡11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗𝑡\sum_{m\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\chi(m)\nu(m)}{m}=\,\prod_{j<i}\sum_{t=0}^{\gamma_{i}}\frac{\chi(p_{j}^{t})\nu(p_{j}^{t})}{p_{j}^{t}}\leqslant\,\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\sum_{t=1}^{\infty}\frac{2t+1}{(1-\delta_{j})p_{j}^{t}}\bigg{)}. (16)

Finally, note that for any p>1𝑝1p>1, we have

∑t⩾12​t+1pt=1(1−p−1)​(3p+2p2+2p3+…)=1(1−p−1)2​(3p−1p2)=3​p−1(p−1)2.subscript𝑡12𝑡1superscript𝑝𝑡11superscript𝑝13𝑝2superscript𝑝22superscript𝑝3…1superscript1superscript𝑝123𝑝1superscript𝑝23𝑝1superscript𝑝12\sum_{t\geqslant 1}\frac{2t+1}{p^{t}}=\frac{1}{(1-p^{-1})}\bigg{(}\frac{3}{p}+\frac{2}{p^{2}}+\frac{2}{p^{3}}+\dots\bigg{)}=\frac{1}{(1-p^{-1})^{2}}\bigg{(}\frac{3}{p}-\frac{1}{p^{2}}\bigg{)}=\frac{3p-1}{(p-1)^{2}}. (17)

This completes the proof of the lemma. ∎

Theorem 3.2 is an almost immediate consequence of Lemmas 3.6 and 3.7.

Proof of Theorem 3.2.

To bound Mi(1)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1M_{i}^{(1)} we apply Lemma 3.6 with k=1𝑘1k=1 and the first inequality in Lemma 3.7. This gives

𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)]⩽∑m​pij∈Nim∣Qi−11pij⋅ν​(m)m⩽1pi−1​∑m∣Qi−1ν​(m)m⩽1pi−1​∏j<i(1+1(1−δj)​(pj−1)),subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖𝑥subscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗subscript𝑁𝑖conditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1⋅1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝜈𝑚𝑚1subscript𝑝𝑖1subscriptconditional𝑚subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈𝑚𝑚1subscript𝑝𝑖1subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖111subscript𝛿𝑗subscript𝑝𝑗1\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)\big{]}\leqslant\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}mp_{i}^{j}\in N_{i}\\ m\mid Q_{i-1}\end{subarray}}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j}}\cdot\frac{\nu(m)}{m}\leqslant\frac{1}{p_{i}-1}\sum_{m\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu(m)}{m}\leqslant\frac{1}{p_{i}-1}\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)}\bigg{)},

as claimed. To bound Mi(2)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2M_{i}^{(2)} we apply Lemma 3.6 with k=2𝑘2k=2 and the second inequality in Lemma 3.7. We obtain

𝔼i−1⁡[αi​(x)2]⩽∑m1​pij1,m2​pij2∈Nim1,m2∣Qi−1pi−(j1+j2)⋅ν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)⩽1(pi−1)2​∑m1,m2∣Qi−1ν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)⩽1(pi−1)2​∏j<i(1+3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2),subscript𝔼𝑖1subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥2subscriptsubscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑚2superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗2subscript𝑁𝑖subscript𝑚1conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑄𝑖1⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12subscriptsubscript𝑚1conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑄𝑖1𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖13subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}_{i-1}\big{[}\alpha_{i}(x)^{2}\big{]}\leqslant\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m_{1}p_{i}^{j_{1}},\,m_{2}p_{i}^{j_{2}}\in N_{i}\\ m_{1},m_{2}\mid Q_{i-1}\end{subarray}}p_{i}^{-(j_{1}+j_{2})}\cdot\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}\\ \leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\sum_{m_{1},m_{2}\mid Q_{i-1}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)},

as required. ∎

4. Proof of the Main Theorem

In order to deduce Theorem 1.1 from Theorems 3.1 and 3.2, it will suffice to show that there is an appropriate choice of M𝑀M and δ1,δ2,…,δnsubscript𝛿1subscript𝛿2…subscript𝛿𝑛\delta_{1},\delta_{2},\dots,\delta_{n}.

Proof of Theorem 1.1.

Let p1,…,pnsubscript𝑝1…subscript𝑝𝑛p_{1},\dots,p_{n} be the primes that divide Q𝑄Q, listed in increasing order, and fix an integer k∗superscript𝑘k^{*}, to be determined later. We set δi=0subscript𝛿𝑖0\delta_{i}=0 for i⩽k∗𝑖superscript𝑘i\leqslant k^{*} and

δi=μ​(pi)−1μ​(pi)=(log⁡pi)3+εpi​(1+(log⁡pi)3+εpi)−1subscript𝛿𝑖𝜇subscript𝑝𝑖1𝜇subscript𝑝𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖3𝜀subscript𝑝𝑖superscript1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖3𝜀subscript𝑝𝑖1\delta_{i}=\frac{\mu(p_{i})-1}{\mu(p_{i})}=\frac{(\log p_{i})^{3+\varepsilon}}{p_{i}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{(\log p_{i})^{3+\varepsilon}}{p_{i}}\bigg{)}^{-1}

for i>k∗𝑖superscript𝑘i>k^{*}. Note that we have δ1,…,δn∈[0,1/2]subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛012\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}\in[0,1/2] if k∗superscript𝑘k^{*} is chosen sufficiently large.

In order to apply Theorem 3.1, we will bound Mi(1)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1M_{i}^{(1)} for each i⩽k∗𝑖superscript𝑘i\leqslant k^{*}, and Mi(2)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2M_{i}^{(2)} for i>k∗𝑖superscript𝑘i>k^{*}. We will do so using Theorem 3.2.

Claim 1.

For any choice of k∗superscript𝑘k^{*}, if M𝑀M is sufficiently large then

∑i⩽k∗Mi(1)⩽14.subscript𝑖superscript𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖114\sum_{i\leqslant k^{*}}M_{i}^{(1)}\leqslant\frac{1}{4}.
Proof of Claim 1.

Note first that ν​(d)=1𝜈𝑑1\nu(d)=1 for every d∈Dk∗𝑑subscript𝐷superscript𝑘d\in D_{k^{*}}, by (8) and our choice of δ1,…,δnsubscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}. Thus, by Theorem 3.2, we have

∑i⩽k∗Mi(1)⩽∑i⩽k∗∑m​pij∈Nipi−j⋅ν​(m)m=∑i⩽k∗∑d∈Ni1d=∑d∈Dk∗1d.subscript𝑖superscript𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1subscript𝑖superscript𝑘subscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗subscript𝑁𝑖⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝜈𝑚𝑚subscript𝑖superscript𝑘subscript𝑑subscript𝑁𝑖1𝑑subscript𝑑subscript𝐷superscript𝑘1𝑑\sum_{i\leqslant k^{*}}M_{i}^{(1)}\leqslant\sum_{i\leqslant k^{*}}\sum_{mp_{i}^{j}\in N_{i}}p_{i}^{-j}\cdot\frac{\nu(m)}{m}=\sum_{i\leqslant k^{*}}\sum_{d\in N_{i}}\frac{1}{d}=\sum_{d\in D_{k^{*}}}\frac{1}{d}.

Now, let 𝒮​(q)𝒮𝑞\mathcal{S}(q) denote the set of q𝑞q-smooth numbers, i.e., numbers all of whose prime factors are at most q𝑞q, and note that Dk∗⊆𝒮​(pk∗)subscript𝐷superscript𝑘𝒮subscript𝑝superscript𝑘D_{k^{*}}\subseteq\mathcal{S}(p_{k^{*}}), and moreover

∑d∈𝒮​(q)1d=∏p⩽q(1+1p−1)<∞subscript𝑑𝒮𝑞1𝑑subscriptproduct𝑝𝑞11𝑝1\sum_{d\in\mathcal{S}(q)}\frac{1}{d}\,=\,\prod_{p\leqslant q}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p-1}\bigg{)}<\infty

for every q𝑞q, where the product is over primes p⩽q𝑝𝑞p\leqslant q. Hence, by choosing M𝑀M to be sufficiently large, it follows that

∑i⩽k∗Mi(1)⩽∑d∈𝒮​(pk∗),d⩾M1d⩽14,subscript𝑖superscript𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖1subscriptformulae-sequence𝑑𝒮subscript𝑝superscript𝑘𝑑𝑀1𝑑14\sum_{i\leqslant k^{*}}M_{i}^{(1)}\leqslant\sum_{d\in\mathcal{S}(p_{k^{*}}),\,d\geqslant M}\frac{1}{d}\,\leqslant\,\frac{1}{4},

as claimed. ∎

Bounding Mi(2)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2M_{i}^{(2)} for i>k∗𝑖superscript𝑘i>k^{*} is only slightly less trivial.

Claim 2.

If k∗superscript𝑘k^{*} is sufficiently large, then

∑i>k∗Mi(2)⩽14.subscript𝑖superscript𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖214\sum_{i>k^{*}}M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\frac{1}{4}.
Proof of Claim 2.

Recall from Theorem 3.2 that

Mi(2)⩽1(pi−1)2​∏j<i(1+3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2)⩽1(pi−1)2​exp⁡(∑j<i3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2).superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖13subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗121superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12subscript𝑗𝑖3subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\prod_{j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\exp\bigg{(}\sum_{j<i}\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}.

Now, by our choice of δ1,…,δnsubscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿𝑛\delta_{1},\dots,\delta_{n}, we have

3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2⩽3pj+O​((log⁡pj)3+ε)pj23subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗123subscript𝑝𝑗𝑂superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗3𝜀superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗2\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\leqslant\frac{3}{p_{j}}+\frac{O\big{(}(\log p_{j})^{3+\varepsilon}\big{)}}{p_{j}^{2}}

for every j<i𝑗𝑖j<i, and hence, using a weak form of Merten’s theorem to deduce that ∑j<i1pj⩽log⁡log⁡pi+O​(1)subscript𝑗𝑖1subscript𝑝𝑗subscript𝑝𝑖𝑂1\sum_{j<i}\frac{1}{p_{j}}\leqslant\log\log p_{i}+O(1), we obtain

Mi(2)⩽1(pi−1)2​exp⁡(3​log⁡log⁡pi+O​(1))⩽C0​(log⁡pi)3pi2superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖123subscript𝑝𝑖𝑂1subscript𝐶0superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖3superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖2M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\exp\big{(}3\log\log p_{i}+O(1)\big{)}\leqslant\frac{C_{0}(\log p_{i})^{3}}{p_{i}^{2}}

for some absolute constant C0>0subscript𝐶00C_{0}>0. It follows that, if i>k∗𝑖superscript𝑘i>k^{*}, then

Mi(2)4​δi​(1−δi)=μ​(pi)24​(μ​(pi)−1)⋅Mi(2)⩽C1pi​(log⁡pi)ε,superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖24subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖⋅𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖24𝜇subscript𝑝𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2subscript𝐶1subscript𝑝𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝜀\frac{M_{i}^{(2)}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}=\frac{\mu(p_{i})^{2}}{4\big{(}\mu(p_{i})-1\big{)}}\cdot M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\frac{C_{1}}{p_{i}(\log p_{i})^{\varepsilon}},

for some absolute constant C1>0subscript𝐶10C_{1}>0. Now, using the prime number theorem π​(x)∼x/log⁡xsimilar-to𝜋𝑥𝑥𝑥\pi(x)\sim x/\log x to crudely bound the sum of the right-hand side over all primes p𝑝p, we obtain

∑p⩾3C1p​(log⁡p)ε=∑t⩾1∑et<p⩽et+1C1p​(log⁡p)ε⩽∑t⩾1C1⋅π​(et+1)et⋅tε⩽∑t⩾1C2t1+ε<∞subscript𝑝3subscript𝐶1𝑝superscript𝑝𝜀subscript𝑡1subscriptsuperscript𝑒𝑡𝑝superscript𝑒𝑡1subscript𝐶1𝑝superscript𝑝𝜀subscript𝑡1⋅subscript𝐶1𝜋superscript𝑒𝑡1⋅superscript𝑒𝑡superscript𝑡𝜀subscript𝑡1subscript𝐶2superscript𝑡1𝜀\sum_{p\geqslant 3}\frac{C_{1}}{p(\log p)^{\varepsilon}}=\sum_{t\geqslant 1}\sum_{e^{t}<p\leqslant e^{t+1}}\frac{C_{1}}{p(\log p)^{\varepsilon}}\leqslant\sum_{t\geqslant 1}\frac{C_{1}\cdot\pi\big{(}e^{t+1}\big{)}}{e^{t}\cdot t^{\varepsilon}}\leqslant\sum_{t\geqslant 1}\frac{C_{2}}{t^{1+\varepsilon}}<\infty

for any ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0. It follows that if k∗superscript𝑘k^{*} is sufficiently large, then

∑i>k∗Mi(2)4​δi​(1−δi)⩽14.subscript𝑖superscript𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖24subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖14\sum_{i>k^{*}}\frac{M_{i}^{(2)}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}\leqslant\frac{1}{4}.

as claimed. ∎

By Claims 1 and 2, it follows that η⩽1/2𝜂12\eta\leqslant 1/2, and hence, by Theorem 3.1, the uncovered set has density at least

12​exp⁡(−4​∑d∈Dν​(d)d).124subscript𝑑𝐷𝜈𝑑𝑑\frac{1}{2}\exp\bigg{(}-4\sum_{d\in D}\frac{\nu(d)}{d}\bigg{)}.

Finally, observe that μ​(d)⩾ν​(d)𝜇𝑑𝜈𝑑\mu(d)\geqslant\nu(d) for every d∈ℕ𝑑ℕd\in\mathbb{N}, since both functions are multiplicative,

μ​(p)=1+(log⁡p)3+εp=11−δi=ν​(p)𝜇𝑝1superscript𝑝3𝜀𝑝11subscript𝛿𝑖𝜈𝑝\mu(p)=1+\frac{(\log p)^{3+\varepsilon}}{p}=\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}=\nu(p)

for p>pk∗𝑝subscript𝑝superscript𝑘p>p_{k^{*}}, and μ​(p)⩾1=ν​(p)𝜇𝑝1𝜈𝑝\mu(p)\geqslant 1=\nu(p) for p⩽pk∗𝑝subscript𝑝superscript𝑘p\leqslant p_{k^{*}}. It follows that the uncovered set has density at least

12​exp⁡(−4​∑d∈Dμ​(d)d)=e−4​C2,124subscript𝑑𝐷𝜇𝑑𝑑superscript𝑒4𝐶2\frac{1}{2}\exp\bigg{(}-4\sum_{d\in D}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\bigg{)}=\frac{e^{-4C}}{2},

as required. ∎

5. The Erdős–Graham Conjecture

As a simple consequence of Theorem 1.1, we will next give a new strengthening of the conjecture of Erdős and Graham [5] mentioned in the Introduction. Recall that in the original conjecture, which was confirmed by Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance and Yu [7] in 2007, the lower bound M𝑀M on n𝑛n was allowed to depend on K𝐾K. The result proved in [7] required such a bound (of the form n>Kω𝑛superscript𝐾𝜔n>K^{\omega} for some ω=ω​(K)→∞𝜔𝜔𝐾→\omega=\omega(K)\rightarrow\infty as K→∞→𝐾K\rightarrow\infty), but gave an asymptotically optimal bound on the density of the uncovered set. Our result gives a non-optimal bound on this density, but does not require n𝑛n to grow with K𝐾K.

Theorem 5.1.

There exists M𝑀M such that for any K⩾1𝐾1K\geqslant 1, there exists δ=δ​(K)>0𝛿𝛿𝐾0\delta=\delta(K)>0 such that the following holds. If A1,…,Aksubscript𝐴1…subscript𝐴𝑘A_{1},\dots,A_{k} are arithmetic progressions with distinct moduli d1,…,dk∈[n,K​n]subscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘𝑛𝐾𝑛d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\in[n,Kn], n⩾M𝑛𝑀n\geqslant M, then the uncovered set R:=ℤ∖⋃i=1kAiassign𝑅ℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘subscript𝐴𝑖R:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}A_{i} has density at least δ𝛿\delta.

Proof.

We apply Theorem 1.1 with ε=1𝜀1\varepsilon=1. In order to prove the corollary, it will suffice to show that there exists a constant C>0𝐶0C>0, depending only on K𝐾K, such that

∑d∈[n,K​n]μ​(d)d⩽Csubscript𝑑𝑛𝐾𝑛𝜇𝑑𝑑𝐶\sum_{d\in[n,Kn]}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant C (18)

for all n∈ℕ𝑛ℕn\in\mathbb{N}. Indeed, if we set δ:=e−4​C/2assign𝛿superscript𝑒4𝐶2\delta:=e^{-4C}/2 then it follows from Theorem 1.1 that, if (18) holds and n⩾M𝑛𝑀n\geqslant M, then the density of R𝑅R is at least δ𝛿\delta.

We will prove, by induction on t⩾0𝑡0t\geqslant 0, that (18) holds for all n⩽2t𝑛superscript2𝑡n\leqslant 2^{t}. This is clearly the case when t=0𝑡0t=0 (assuming that C=C​(K)𝐶𝐶𝐾C=C(K) is sufficiently large), so let t⩾1𝑡1t\geqslant 1, and let us assume that (18) holds for all n⩽2t−1𝑛superscript2𝑡1n\leqslant 2^{t-1}. We will use the following telescopic series, which holds for any i0subscript𝑖0i_{0} and d∣Qconditional𝑑𝑄d\mid Q by the definition (2) of μ𝜇\mu:

μ​(d)𝜇𝑑\displaystyle\mu(d) =μ​(gcd⁡(d,Qi0))+∑i>i0(μ​(gcd⁡(d,Qi))−μ​(gcd⁡(d,Qi−1)))absent𝜇𝑑subscript𝑄subscript𝑖0subscript𝑖subscript𝑖0𝜇𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖𝜇𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖1\displaystyle=\mu\big{(}\gcd(d,Q_{i_{0}})\big{)}+\sum_{i>i_{0}}\Big{(}\mu\big{(}\gcd(d,Q_{i})\big{)}-\mu\big{(}\gcd(d,Q_{i-1})\big{)}\Big{)}
=μ​(gcd⁡(d,Qi0))+∑pi​∣d,i>​i0(μ​(pi)−1)​μ​(gcd⁡(d,Qi−1)).absent𝜇𝑑subscript𝑄subscript𝑖0subscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖ket𝑑𝑖subscript𝑖0𝜇subscript𝑝𝑖1𝜇𝑑subscript𝑄𝑖1\displaystyle=\mu\big{(}\gcd(d,Q_{i_{0}})\big{)}+\sum_{p_{i}\mid d,\,i>i_{0}}\big{(}\mu(p_{i})-1\big{)}\mu\big{(}\gcd(d,Q_{i-1})\big{)}.

Summing over d∈[n,K​n]𝑑𝑛𝐾𝑛d\in[n,Kn], we obtain

∑d∈[n,K​n]μ​(d)d⩽∑d∈[n,K​n]μ​(Qi0)d+∑i>i0μ​(pi)−1pi​∑d∈[n/pi,K​n/pi]μ​(d)d.subscript𝑑𝑛𝐾𝑛𝜇𝑑𝑑subscript𝑑𝑛𝐾𝑛𝜇subscript𝑄subscript𝑖0𝑑subscript𝑖subscript𝑖0𝜇subscript𝑝𝑖1subscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑑𝑛subscript𝑝𝑖𝐾𝑛subscript𝑝𝑖𝜇𝑑𝑑\sum_{d\in[n,Kn]}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\sum_{d\in[n,Kn]}\frac{\mu(Q_{i_{0}})}{d}+\sum_{i>i_{0}}\frac{\mu(p_{i})-1}{p_{i}}\sum_{d\in[n/p_{i},Kn/p_{i}]}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}.

Now, since n/p⩽2t−1𝑛𝑝superscript2𝑡1n/p\leqslant 2^{t-1} for every prime p𝑝p, it follows by the induction hypothesis that

∑d∈[n,K​n]μ​(d)d⩽μ​(Qi0)​(log⁡K+1)+C​∑i>i0μ​(pi)−1pi.subscript𝑑𝑛𝐾𝑛𝜇𝑑𝑑𝜇subscript𝑄subscript𝑖0𝐾1𝐶subscript𝑖subscript𝑖0𝜇subscript𝑝𝑖1subscript𝑝𝑖\sum_{d\in[n,Kn]}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\mu(Q_{i_{0}})\big{(}\log K+1\big{)}+C\sum_{i>i_{0}}\frac{\mu(p_{i})-1}{p_{i}}.

Finally, note that the sum over all primes

∑p​ primeμ​(p)−1p=∑p​ prime(log⁡p)4p2subscript𝑝 prime𝜇𝑝1𝑝subscript𝑝 primesuperscript𝑝4superscript𝑝2\sum_{p\text{ prime}}\frac{\mu(p)-1}{p}=\sum_{p\text{ prime}}\frac{(\log p)^{4}}{p^{2}}

converges, and therefore

∑d∈[n,K​n]μ​(d)d⩽μ​(Qi0)​(log⁡K+1)+C2⩽Csubscript𝑑𝑛𝐾𝑛𝜇𝑑𝑑𝜇subscript𝑄subscript𝑖0𝐾1𝐶2𝐶\sum_{d\in[n,Kn]}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\mu(Q_{i_{0}})\big{(}\log K+1\big{)}+\frac{C}{2}\leqslant C

if i0subscript𝑖0i_{0} and then C=C​(K)𝐶𝐶𝐾C=C(K) are chosen sufficiently large, as required. ∎

6. A general method of applying the sieve

In this section we describe a practical method of applying our method to problems involving covering systems, such as Schinzel’s conjecture and the Erdős–Selfridge problem. More precisely, we will show how one can choose the constants δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i} sequentially and optimally via a simple recursion which may be run on a computer. We will also give a simple criterion (see Theorem 6.1, below) which we prove is sufficient to deduce that the collection 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers. Combining these (that is, running the recursion until the criterion is satisfied), we reduce the problems to finite calculations, which in some cases are tractable. To demonstrate the power of this approach, we will use it in Sections 7–9 to prove Theorems 1.2 and 1.4, and to significantly improve the bound on M𝑀M in Hough’s theorem.

We begin by defining a sequence of numbers fk=fk​(𝒜)subscript𝑓𝑘subscript𝑓𝑘𝒜f_{k}=f_{k}(\mathcal{A}), which will (roughly speaking) encode how “well” we are doing after k𝑘k steps of our sieve. We remark that, from now on, we will perform a step of the sieve for every prime, whether or not it divides Q𝑄Q. We will therefore write pksubscript𝑝𝑘p_{k} for the k𝑘kth prime, i.e., p1=2subscript𝑝12p_{1}=2, p2=3subscript𝑝23p_{2}=3, etc. Fix i0∈ℕsubscript𝑖0ℕi_{0}\in\mathbb{N}, and define

fk=fk​(𝒜):=κμk​∏i0<i⩽k(1+3​pi−1(1−δi)​(pi−1)2)subscript𝑓𝑘subscript𝑓𝑘𝒜assign𝜅subscript𝜇𝑘subscriptproductsubscript𝑖0𝑖𝑘13subscript𝑝𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12f_{k}=f_{k}(\mathcal{A}):=\frac{\kappa}{\mu_{k}}\prod_{i_{0}<i\leqslant k}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{i}-1}{(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)} (19)

for each k⩾i0𝑘subscript𝑖0k\geqslant i_{0}, where

μk:=1−∑i⩽kℙi⁡(Bi),assignsubscript𝜇𝑘1subscript𝑖𝑘subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖\mu_{k}:=1-\sum_{i\leqslant k}\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i}),

and κ>0𝜅0\kappa>0 and i0∈ℕsubscript𝑖0ℕi_{0}\in\mathbb{N} are chosen so that

Mk(2)⩽κ(pk−1)2​∏i0<i<k(1+3​pi−1(1−δi)​(pi−1)2)=μk−1​fk−1(pk−1)2subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑘𝜅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑘12subscriptproductsubscript𝑖0𝑖𝑘13subscript𝑝𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12subscript𝜇𝑘1subscript𝑓𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑘12M^{(2)}_{k}\leqslant\frac{\kappa}{(p_{k}-1)^{2}}\prod_{i_{0}<i<k}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{i}-1}{(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}=\frac{\mu_{k-1}f_{k-1}}{(p_{k}-1)^{2}} (20)

for every k>i0𝑘subscript𝑖0k>i_{0}. For example, by Theorem 3.2,

κ=∏i⩽i0,pi∣Q(1+3​pi−1(1−δi)​(pi−1)2)𝜅subscriptproduct𝑖subscript𝑖0conditionalsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑄13subscript𝑝𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12\kappa=\prod_{i\leqslant i_{0},\,p_{i}\mid Q}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{i}-1}{(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)} (21)

is a valid choice, although in some cases we can prove a stronger bound. We remark that, in practice, we will choose the constants κ𝜅\kappa and δ1,…,δi0subscript𝛿1…subscript𝛿subscript𝑖0\delta_{1},\ldots,\delta_{i_{0}}, and show that (20) holds for any sequence (δi0+1,…,δn)subscript𝛿subscript𝑖01…subscript𝛿𝑛(\delta_{i_{0}+1},\ldots,\delta_{n}). We will then choose each subsequent δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i} so as to minimize fisubscript𝑓𝑖f_{i}.

The following theorem gives a sufficient condition (at step k𝑘k) for our sieve to be successful. The bound we prove gives an almost optimal termination criterion when k𝑘k is large.

Theorem 6.1.

Let k⩾10𝑘10k\geqslant 10. If μk>0subscript𝜇𝑘0\mu_{k}>0 and fk​(𝒜)⩽(log⁡k+log⁡log⁡k−3)2​ksubscript𝑓𝑘𝒜superscript𝑘𝑘32𝑘f_{k}(\mathcal{A})\leqslant(\log k+\log\log k-3)^{2}k, then the system of arithmetic progressions 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover ℤℤ\mathbb{Z}.

In this section, it will be convenient to define, for each i∈ℕ𝑖ℕi\in\mathbb{N},

ai=3​pi−1(pi−1)2andbi=14​(pi−1)2.formulae-sequencesubscript𝑎𝑖3subscript𝑝𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12andsubscript𝑏𝑖14superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12a_{i}=\frac{3p_{i}-1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\qquad\text{and}\qquad b_{i}=\frac{1}{4(p_{i}-1)^{2}}. (22)

The first step in the proof of Theorem 6.1 is the following simple (but key) lemma.

Lemma 6.2.

Let i>i0𝑖subscript𝑖0i>i_{0}, and assume that μi−1>0subscript𝜇𝑖10\mu_{i-1}>0. If bi​fi−1<δi​(1−δi)subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖b_{i}f_{i-1}<\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i}), then μi>0subscript𝜇𝑖0\mu_{i}>0, and

fi⩽(1+ai1−δi)​(1−bi​fi−1δi​(1−δi))−1​fi−1.subscript𝑓𝑖1subscript𝑎𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖superscript1subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝑓𝑖1f_{i}\,\leqslant\bigg{(}1+\frac{a_{i}}{1-\delta_{i}}\bigg{)}\bigg{(}1-\frac{b_{i}f_{i-1}}{\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}\bigg{)}^{-1}f_{i-1}. (23)
Proof.

Recall from Lemma 3.3 and (20), that

μi−1−μi=ℙi⁡(Bi)⩽Mi(2)4​δi​(1−δi)⩽μi−1​fi−14​δi​(1−δi)​(pi−1)2.subscript𝜇𝑖1subscript𝜇𝑖subscriptℙ𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑖4subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖subscript𝜇𝑖1subscript𝑓𝑖14subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12\mu_{i-1}-\mu_{i}\,=\,\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i}(B_{i})\,\leqslant\,\frac{M^{(2)}_{i}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}\,\leqslant\,\frac{\mu_{i-1}f_{i-1}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}. (24)

It follows that μi⩾μi−1​(1−bi​fi−1/δi​(1−δi))>0subscript𝜇𝑖subscript𝜇𝑖11subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖0\mu_{i}\geqslant\mu_{i-1}\big{(}1-b_{i}f_{i-1}/\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})\big{)}>0, and moreover

fifi−1subscript𝑓𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1\displaystyle\frac{f_{i}}{f_{i-1}} =μi−1μi​(1+3​pi−1(1−δi)​(pi−1)2)absentsubscript𝜇𝑖1subscript𝜇𝑖13subscript𝑝𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12\displaystyle\,=\,\frac{\mu_{i-1}}{\mu_{i}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{i}-1}{(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}
⩽(1+3​pi−1(1−δi)​(pi−1)2)​(1−fi−14​δi​(1−δi)​(pi−1)2)−1,absent13subscript𝑝𝑖11subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12superscript1subscript𝑓𝑖14subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖121\displaystyle\,\leqslant\,\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{i}-1}{(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}\bigg{(}1-\frac{f_{i-1}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}^{-1},

as claimed. ∎

To deduce Theorem 6.1, we will use the main result (which is also the title) of [2], which states that for each k⩾2𝑘2k\geqslant 2, the k𝑘kth prime is greater than k​(log⁡k+log⁡log⁡k−1)𝑘𝑘𝑘1k(\log k+\log\log k-1).

Proof of Theorem 6.1.

We are required to show that μn>0subscript𝜇𝑛0\mu_{n}>0; to do so, we will use Lemma 6.2 to show, by induction on i𝑖i, that μi>0subscript𝜇𝑖0\mu_{i}>0 for every k⩽i⩽n𝑘𝑖𝑛k\leqslant i\leqslant n. As part of the induction, we will also prove that fi⩽λi2​isubscript𝑓𝑖superscriptsubscript𝜆𝑖2𝑖f_{i}\leqslant\lambda_{i}^{2}i for each k⩽i⩽n𝑘𝑖𝑛k\leqslant i\leqslant n, where λi=log⁡i+log⁡log⁡i−3subscript𝜆𝑖𝑖𝑖3\lambda_{i}=\log i+\log\log i-3.

Note that the base case, i=k𝑖𝑘i=k, follows from our assumptions, and set δi=1/2subscript𝛿𝑖12\delta_{i}=1/2 for each k<i⩽n𝑘𝑖𝑛k<i\leqslant n. By Lemma 6.2, for the induction step it will suffice to show that 4​bi​fi−1<14subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖114b_{i}f_{i-1}<1 and

λi−12​(i−1)​(1+2​ai1−4​bi​fi−1)⩽λi2​i,superscriptsubscript𝜆𝑖12𝑖112subscript𝑎𝑖14subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝜆𝑖2𝑖\lambda_{i-1}^{2}(i-1)\bigg{(}\frac{1+2a_{i}}{1-4b_{i}f_{i-1}}\bigg{)}\leqslant\lambda_{i}^{2}i,

where aisubscript𝑎𝑖a_{i} and bisubscript𝑏𝑖b_{i} were defined in (22). We claim that, writing λ=λi−1𝜆subscript𝜆𝑖1\lambda=\lambda_{i-1}, we have

2​ai⩽6​(λ+2)​i+4(λ+2)2​i2,4​bi​fi−1⩽λ2​(i−1)(λ+2)2​i2<1,andλi2​i⩾λ​(λ​i+2).formulae-sequenceformulae-sequence2subscript𝑎𝑖6𝜆2𝑖4superscript𝜆22superscript𝑖24subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1superscript𝜆2𝑖1superscript𝜆22superscript𝑖21andsuperscriptsubscript𝜆𝑖2𝑖𝜆𝜆𝑖22a_{i}\leqslant\frac{6(\lambda+2)i+4}{(\lambda+2)^{2}i^{2}},\qquad 4b_{i}f_{i-1}\leqslant\frac{\lambda^{2}(i-1)}{(\lambda+2)^{2}i^{2}}<1,\qquad\text{and}\qquad\lambda_{i}^{2}i\geqslant\lambda(\lambda i+2).

To see these inequalities, note that pi⩾(λi+2)​isubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝜆𝑖2𝑖p_{i}\geqslant(\lambda_{i}+2)i for all i⩾k𝑖𝑘i\geqslant k, by the result of [2] stated above, and λi−λi−1⩾log⁡(i/(i−1))>1/isubscript𝜆𝑖subscript𝜆𝑖1𝑖𝑖11𝑖\lambda_{i}-\lambda_{i-1}\geqslant\log(i/(i-1))>1/i, so pi−1⩾(λi−1+1/i+2)​i−1=(λ+2)​isubscript𝑝𝑖1subscript𝜆𝑖11𝑖2𝑖1𝜆2𝑖p_{i}-1\geqslant(\lambda_{i-1}+1/i+2)i-1=(\lambda+2)i.

It is therefore sufficient to show that

λ2​(i−1)​(1+6​(λ+2)​i+4(λ+2)2​i2)⩽λ​(λ​i+2)​(1−λ2​(i−1)(λ+2)2​i2),superscript𝜆2𝑖116𝜆2𝑖4superscript𝜆22superscript𝑖2𝜆𝜆𝑖21superscript𝜆2𝑖1superscript𝜆22superscript𝑖2\lambda^{2}(i-1)\bigg{(}1+\frac{6(\lambda+2)i+4}{(\lambda+2)^{2}i^{2}}\bigg{)}\leqslant\lambda(\lambda i+2)\bigg{(}1-\frac{\lambda^{2}(i-1)}{(\lambda+2)^{2}i^{2}}\bigg{)},

which (after multiplying by (λ+2)2​i2/λsuperscript𝜆22superscript𝑖2𝜆(\lambda+2)^{2}i^{2}/\lambda, expanding and rearranging) becomes

0⩽8​i2+λ3​i+4​λ2​i+8​λ​i+2​λ2+4​λ.08superscript𝑖2superscript𝜆3𝑖4superscript𝜆2𝑖8𝜆𝑖2superscript𝜆24𝜆0\leqslant 8i^{2}+\lambda^{3}i+4\lambda^{2}i+8\lambda i+2\lambda^{2}+4\lambda.

This clearly holds for all λ>0𝜆0\lambda>0 and i∈ℕ𝑖ℕi\in\mathbb{N}, and so the theorem follows. ∎

Now, combining Theorem 6.1 and Lemma 6.2, we can deduce from (sufficiently strong) bounds on fksubscript𝑓𝑘f_{k}, for any k∈ℕ𝑘ℕk\in\mathbb{N}, that the uncovered set is non-empty. To do so, observe first that, given fi−1subscript𝑓𝑖1f_{i-1}, the bound on fisubscript𝑓𝑖f_{i} given by Lemma 6.2 is just a function of a single δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i}. Elementary calculus shows that the optimal choice of δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i} occurs when

δi=1+ai1+1+ai​(1+ai)/(bi​fi−1).subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝑎𝑖11subscript𝑎𝑖1subscript𝑎𝑖subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1\delta_{i}=\frac{1+a_{i}}{1+\sqrt{1+a_{i}(1+a_{i})/(b_{i}f_{i-1})}}. (25)

This expression for δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i} allows for very fast numerical computation of the bounds on the fisubscript𝑓𝑖f_{i}.

Let us therefore, for each i∈ℕ𝑖ℕi\in\mathbb{N}, define gisubscript𝑔𝑖g_{i} to be the largest value of fi​(𝒜)subscript𝑓𝑖𝒜f_{i}(\mathcal{A}) such that, by repeatedly applying the recursion (23) with δjsubscript𝛿𝑗\delta_{j} given by (25), we eventually satisfy the conditions of Theorem 6.1 for some k⩾10𝑘10k\geqslant 10. In Table 1 we list the bounds on gksubscript𝑔𝑘g_{k} given by performing this calculation, which was implemented as follows: starting with a potential value of f3subscript𝑓3f_{3}, we ran the iteration given in (23) using the value of δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i} given in (25) until either the conditions of Theorem 6.1 were satisfied, or the condition bi​fi−1<δi​(1−δi)subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖b_{i}f_{i-1}<\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i}) failed. The optimal value g3subscript𝑔3g_{3} of f3subscript𝑓3f_{3} was determined by binary chop, and the other bounds gksubscript𝑔𝑘g_{k} were read off by taking the largest successful f3subscript𝑓3f_{3} and listing the corresponding bounds on fksubscript𝑓𝑘f_{k}.

Let us state, for future reference, the conclusion of this section.

Corollary 6.3.

If fk​(𝒜)⩽gksubscript𝑓𝑘𝒜subscript𝑔𝑘f_{k}(\mathcal{A})\leqslant g_{k} for some k∈ℕ𝑘ℕk\in\mathbb{N}, then the system of arithmetic progressions 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover ℤℤ\mathbb{Z}.

We remark that the bound on g1subscript𝑔1g_{1} given by our sieve is less than 111, and for this reason we are unable to resolve the Erdős–Selfridge problem.

7. The Erdős–Selfridge Problem

In this section we will prove Theorem 1.4, which is a simple consequence of the method described in the previous section. First, however, let us show how to prove the following (only slightly weaker) theorem, which was first proved by Hough and Nielsen [9].

Theorem 7.1.

Let 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} be a finite collection of arithmetic progressions with distinct moduli, none of which is divisible by 222 or 333. Then 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers.

Proof.

Set i0=2subscript𝑖02i_{0}=2 and κ=1𝜅1\kappa=1, note that μ1=μ2=1subscript𝜇1subscript𝜇21\mu_{1}=\mu_{2}=1, since there are no moduli divisible by 2 or 3, and recall from (21) that this is a valid choice of κ𝜅\kappa, by Theorem 3.2.
