Recalling from (19) that f2=κ/μ2subscript𝑓2𝜅subscript𝜇2f_{2}=\kappa/\mu_{2}, and using Table 1, we see that f2=1<1.26<g2subscript𝑓211.26subscript𝑔2f_{2}=1<1.26<g_{2}, and hence, by Corollary 6.3, the system 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers. ∎

We remark that we did not actually need the full strength of Corollary 6.3 to prove this theorem; in fact, we could have just run our sieve with δ1=⋯=δn=1/4subscript𝛿1⋯subscript𝛿𝑛14\delta_{1}=\cdots=\delta_{n}=1/4, say, and applied Theorems 3.1 and 3.2, together with Theorem 6.1. In order to prove Theorem 1.4, we will need a slightly more complicated version of the proof above.

Proof of Theorem 1.4.

By Theorem 7.1, we may assume that Q=lcm⁡(D)𝑄lcm𝐷Q=\operatorname{lcm}(D) satisfies Q=3​Q′𝑄3superscript𝑄′Q=3Q^{\prime}, where Q′superscript𝑄′Q^{\prime} is not divisible by 222, 333 or 555. Observe that μ1=1subscript𝜇11\mu_{1}=1, and that μ3=μ2⩾2/3subscript𝜇3subscript𝜇223\mu_{3}=\mu_{2}\geqslant 2/3, by the (trivial) first moment bound M2(1)⩽1/3superscriptsubscript𝑀2113M_{2}^{(1)}\leqslant 1/3, and since there are no moduli divisible by 2 or 5. Set i0=3subscript𝑖03i_{0}=3, δ1=δ2=δ3=0subscript𝛿1subscript𝛿2subscript𝛿30\delta_{1}=\delta_{2}=\delta_{3}=0, and κ=2𝜅2\kappa=2. To see that this is a valid choice of κ𝜅\kappa, we need to improve (21) slightly, using the fact that 32∤Qnot-dividessuperscript32𝑄3^{2}\nmid Q. To be precise, in the proof of Lemma 3.7, in the last expression in (16), when j=2𝑗2j=2 we only need to include the term t=1𝑡1t=1 in the sum. Keeping the rest of the proof of Theorem 3.2 the same, this implies that

Mi(2)⩽1(pi−1)2​(1+3(1−δ2)​p2)​∏3<j<i(1+3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2),superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12131subscript𝛿2subscript𝑝2subscriptproduct3𝑗𝑖13subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\frac{1}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3}{(1-\delta_{2})p_{2}}\bigg{)}\prod_{3<j<i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)},

and hence (20) holds with κ=1+3/p2=2𝜅13subscript𝑝22\kappa=1+3/p_{2}=2, as claimed. Using Table 1, it follows that

f3=κμ3⩽3<3.007⩽g3,subscript𝑓3𝜅subscript𝜇333.007subscript𝑔3f_{3}=\frac{\kappa}{\mu_{3}}\leqslant 3<3.007\leqslant g_{3},

and therefore, by Corollary 6.3, the system 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers. ∎

8. The Minimum Modulus Problem

In this section we improve the bound on the minimum modulus given in [8, Theorem 1].

Theorem 8.1.

Let 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} be a finite collection of arithmetic progressions with distinct moduli d1,…,dk⩾616000subscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘616000d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\geqslant 616000. Then 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers.

Proof.

We apply Theorem 3.1, using the first moment Mi(1)subscriptsuperscript𝑀1𝑖M^{(1)}_{i} and setting δi=0subscript𝛿𝑖0\delta_{i}=0 for i⩽51𝑖51i\leqslant 51 (note that p51=233subscript𝑝51233p_{51}=233). After the first 51 primes we have

μ51⩾1−∑d⩾616000p51​-smooth1d⩾ 0.654258subscript𝜇511subscript𝑑616000subscript𝑝51-smooth1𝑑0.654258\mu_{51}\geqslant 1-\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}d\geqslant 616000\\ p_{51}\text{-smooth}\end{subarray}}\frac{1}{d}\,\geqslant\,0.654258 (26)

and

f51⩽1μ51​∏j⩽51(1+3​pj−1(pj−1)2)⩽ 886.56.subscript𝑓511subscript𝜇51subscriptproduct𝑗5113subscript𝑝𝑗1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12886.56f_{51}\leqslant\frac{1}{\mu_{51}}\prod_{j\leqslant 51}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}\leqslant\,886.56.

For 51<i⩽5100051𝑖5100051<i\leqslant 51000 we apply the second moment bound using

Mi(2)⩽M^i(2):=∑m1​pij,m2​pik⩾616000m1,m2​pi−1​-smoothp−j−k⋅ν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2).subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑖subscriptsuperscript^𝑀2𝑖assignsubscriptsubscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗subscript𝑚2superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑘616000subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑝𝑖1-smooth⋅superscript𝑝𝑗𝑘𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2M^{(2)}_{i}\leqslant\hat{M}^{(2)}_{i}:=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m_{1}p_{i}^{j},m_{2}p_{i}^{k}\geqslant 616000\\ m_{1},m_{2}\ p_{i-1}\text{-smooth}\end{subarray}}p^{-j-k}\cdot\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}. (27)

The values of δisubscript𝛿𝑖\delta_{i} were not optimized, but instead defined by the following equation

δi=(1−1pi)⋅1+ai1+1+4​μ^i​ai​(1+ai)/M^i(2),subscript𝛿𝑖⋅11subscript𝑝𝑖1subscript𝑎𝑖114subscript^𝜇𝑖subscript𝑎𝑖1subscript𝑎𝑖superscriptsubscript^𝑀𝑖2\delta_{i}=\bigg{(}1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{p_{i}}}\bigg{)}\cdot\frac{1+a_{i}}{1+\sqrt{1+4\hat{\mu}_{i}a_{i}(1+a_{i})/\hat{M}_{i}^{(2)}}},

which is based on (25), but with bi​fi−1subscript𝑏𝑖subscript𝑓𝑖1b_{i}f_{i-1} replaced by the bound on Mi(2)/(4​μi)superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖24subscript𝜇𝑖M_{i}^{(2)}/(4\mu_{i}) implied by (27). The (rather arbitrary) factor of (1−1/pi)11subscript𝑝𝑖(1-1/\sqrt{p_{i}}) was included to improve the bounds obtained, and μ^isubscript^𝜇𝑖\hat{\mu}_{i} is the lower bound on μisubscript𝜇𝑖\mu_{i} defined inductively by

μ^i=μ^i−1−M^i(2)4​δi​(1−δi).subscript^𝜇𝑖subscript^𝜇𝑖1superscriptsubscript^𝑀𝑖24subscript𝛿𝑖1subscript𝛿𝑖\hat{\mu}_{i}=\hat{\mu}_{i-1}-\frac{\hat{M}_{i}^{(2)}}{4\delta_{i}(1-\delta_{i})}.

Finally, after processing p51000=625187>616000subscript𝑝51000625187616000p_{51000}=625187>616000 we calculated the bound f51000⩽5589593subscript𝑓510005589593f_{51000}\leqslant 5589593 from (19) using i0=0subscript𝑖00i_{0}=0 and κ=1𝜅1\kappa=1. This is less than the bound g51000subscript𝑔51000g_{51000} given in Table 1, and hence, by Corollary 6.3, the system 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A} does not cover the integers.

It only remains to describe an efficient way of calculating the expressions (26) and (27). For (26) we note that the sum of 1/d1𝑑1/d over all pisubscript𝑝𝑖p_{i}-smooth d𝑑d is given by the product

∑d​pi​-smooth1d=∏j⩽i(1+1pj−1)subscript𝑑subscript𝑝𝑖-smooth1𝑑subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖11subscript𝑝𝑗1\sum_{d\ p_{i}\text{-smooth}}\frac{1}{d}\,=\,\prod_{j\leqslant i}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p_{j}-1}\bigg{)}

and the sum in (26) can then be calculated by subtracting the finite sum of 1/d1𝑑1/d over all pisubscript𝑝𝑖p_{i}-smooth d<616000𝑑616000d<616000. For (27) the procedure is somewhat more complicated. First we define

Θi​(s,t):=∑m1⩾s,m2⩾tm1,m2​pi​-smoothν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2),assignsubscriptΘ𝑖𝑠𝑡subscriptformulae-sequencesubscript𝑚1𝑠subscript𝑚2𝑡subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑝𝑖-smooth𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2\Theta_{i}(s,t):=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}m_{1}\geqslant s,\,m_{2}\geqslant t\\ m_{1},m_{2}\ p_{i}\text{-smooth}\end{subarray}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})},

which can be calculated inductively using the identity

Θi​(s,t)=Θi−1​(s,t)+11−δi​∑j,k⩾0,j+k>0pi−max⁡{j,k}⋅Θi−1​(⌈s/pij⌉,⌈t/pik⌉),subscriptΘ𝑖𝑠𝑡subscriptΘ𝑖1𝑠𝑡11subscript𝛿𝑖subscriptformulae-sequence𝑗𝑘0𝑗𝑘0⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝑘subscriptΘ𝑖1𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑘\Theta_{i}(s,t)=\Theta_{i-1}(s,t)+\frac{1}{1-\delta_{i}}\sum_{j,k\geqslant 0,\,j+k>0}p_{i}^{-\max\{j,k\}}\cdot\Theta_{i-1}\big{(}\lceil s/p_{i}^{j}\rceil,\lceil t/p_{i}^{k}\rceil\big{)},

which, despite its appearance, can be calculated as a finite sum. Indeed, ⌈s/pij⌉=1𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗1\lceil s/p_{i}^{j}\rceil=1 for sufficiently large j𝑗j, and so there are only finitely many terms Θi−1​(s′,t′)subscriptΘ𝑖1superscript𝑠′superscript𝑡′\Theta_{i-1}(s^{\prime},t^{\prime}) that occur, and these are multiplied (when s′superscript𝑠′s^{\prime} or t′=1superscript𝑡′1t^{\prime}=1) by geometric series that can be summed exactly. Finally, the calculation of

Mi(2)⩽∑j,k⩾1pi−j−k⋅Θi−1​(⌈K/pij⌉,⌈K/pik⌉),subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑖subscript𝑗𝑘1⋅superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝑘subscriptΘ𝑖1𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑘M^{(2)}_{i}\leqslant\sum_{j,k\geqslant 1}p_{i}^{-j-k}\cdot\Theta_{i-1}\big{(}\lceil K/p_{i}^{j}\rceil,\lceil K/p_{i}^{k}\rceil\big{)},

where K=616000𝐾616000K=616000, can similarly be reduced to a finite sum. ∎

9. Schinzel’s Conjecture

In this section we will use the method of Section 6 to prove Schinzel’s Conjecture [10], which we restate here for convenience.

Theorem 9.1.

If 1<d1<d2<⋯<dk1subscript𝑑1subscript𝑑2⋯subscript𝑑𝑘1<d_{1}<d_{2}<\dots<d_{k} are the moduli of a finite collection of arithmetic progressions that covers the integers, then di∣djconditionalsubscript𝑑𝑖subscript𝑑𝑗d_{i}\mid d_{j} for some i<j𝑖𝑗i<j.

We will argue by contradiction, assuming that we have a set {d1,…,dk}subscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘\{d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\} of moduli of a covering system of the integers that forms an antichain under divisibility. We call an antichain of natural numbers p𝑝p-smooth if all its elements are p𝑝p-smooth, i.e., have no prime factor greater than p𝑝p. In order to apply our sieve, we will need the following three simple lemmas about 555-smooth antichains.

Lemma 9.2.

If D𝐷D is a 555-smooth antichain containing no prime power, then

∑d∈D1d⩽13,subscript𝑑𝐷1𝑑13\sum_{d\in D}\frac{1}{d}\,\leqslant\,\frac{1}{3},

with equality if and only if D={6,10,15}𝐷61015D=\{6,10,15\}.

Proof.

Suppose first that D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime} is a 333-smooth antichain (possibly containing a prime power), and observe that

D′={2a1​3b1,2a2​3b2,…,2ak​3bk}superscript𝐷′superscript2subscript𝑎1superscript3subscript𝑏1superscript2subscript𝑎2superscript3subscript𝑏2…superscript2subscript𝑎𝑘superscript3subscript𝑏𝑘D^{\prime}=\big{\{}2^{a_{1}}3^{b_{1}},2^{a_{2}}3^{b_{2}},\dots,2^{a_{k}}3^{b_{k}}\big{\}}

for some a1>a2>⋯>aksubscript𝑎1subscript𝑎2⋯subscript𝑎𝑘a_{1}>a_{2}>\dots>a_{k} and b1<b2<⋯<bksubscript𝑏1subscript𝑏2⋯subscript𝑏𝑘b_{1}<b_{2}<\dots<b_{k}. We claim that ∑d∈D′1dsubscript𝑑superscript𝐷′1𝑑\sum_{d\in D^{\prime}}\frac{1}{d} is maximized when D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime} is ‘compressed’, that is, ai=ai+1+1subscript𝑎𝑖subscript𝑎𝑖11a_{i}=a_{i+1}+1 and bi+1=bi+1subscript𝑏𝑖1subscript𝑏𝑖1b_{i+1}=b_{i}+1 for all i∈[k−1]𝑖delimited-[]𝑘1i\in[k-1]. Indeed, if ai>ai+1+1subscript𝑎𝑖subscript𝑎𝑖11a_{i}>a_{i+1}+1 then we can reduce aisubscript𝑎𝑖a_{i}, and if bi+1>bi+1subscript𝑏𝑖1subscript𝑏𝑖1b_{i+1}>b_{i}+1 then we can reduce bi+1subscript𝑏𝑖1b_{i+1}, in each case increasing ∑d∈D′1dsubscript𝑑superscript𝐷′1𝑑\sum_{d\in D^{\prime}}\frac{1}{d} while maintaining the property that D′superscript𝐷′D^{\prime} is an antichain.

Now, let us write the 5-smooth antichain D𝐷D as a union of sets of the form {5i​d:d∈Di}conditional-setsuperscript5𝑖𝑑𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖\{5^{i}d:d\in D_{i}\}, where each Disubscript𝐷𝑖D_{i} is a (possibly empty) 3-smooth antichain. Since D𝐷D contains no prime power, neither can D0subscript𝐷0D_{0}, so ∑d∈D01dsubscript𝑑subscript𝐷01𝑑\sum_{d\in D_{0}}\frac{1}{d} is maximized when D0={2a⋅3,2a−1⋅32,…,2⋅3a}subscript𝐷0⋅superscript2𝑎3⋅superscript2𝑎1superscript32…⋅2superscript3𝑎D_{0}=\{2^{a}\cdot 3,2^{a-1}\cdot 3^{2},\dots,2\cdot 3^{a}\}. In this case a simple calculation shows that ∑d∈D01d=2−a−3−asubscript𝑑subscript𝐷01𝑑superscript2𝑎superscript3𝑎\sum_{d\in D_{0}}\frac{1}{d}=2^{-a}-3^{-a}, which attains a unique maximum when a=1𝑎1a=1. Thus ∑d∈D01d⩽16subscript𝑑subscript𝐷01𝑑16\sum_{d\in D_{0}}\frac{1}{d}\leqslant\frac{1}{6}, with equality if and only if D0={6}subscript𝐷06D_{0}=\{6\}.

Next, observe that 1∉Di1subscript𝐷𝑖1\not\in D_{i} for every i⩾1𝑖1i\geqslant 1, and therefore ∑d∈Di1dsubscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖1𝑑\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{1}{d} is maximized when Di={2a,2a−1​31,…,3a}subscript𝐷𝑖superscript2𝑎superscript2𝑎1superscript31…superscript3𝑎D_{i}=\{2^{a},2^{a-1}3^{1},\dots,3^{a}\}, with a⩾1𝑎1a\geqslant 1. In this case we have ∑d∈Di1d=6​(2−1−a−3−1−a)subscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖1𝑑6superscript21𝑎superscript31𝑎\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{1}{d}=6(2^{-1-a}-3^{-1-a}) and the unique extremal case is a=1𝑎1a=1, so ∑d∈Di1d⩽56subscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖1𝑑56\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{1}{d}\leqslant\frac{5}{6}, with equality if and only if Di={2,3}subscript𝐷𝑖23D_{i}=\{2,3\}. If we additionally assume that Di≠{2,3}subscript𝐷𝑖23D_{i}\neq\{2,3\}, then ∑d∈Di1d⩽1118subscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖1𝑑1118\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{1}{d}\leqslant\frac{11}{18}, with the unique maximum occurring for the (uncompressed) antichain Di={2,9}subscript𝐷𝑖29D_{i}=\{2,9\}.

Finally, if D1={2,3}subscript𝐷123D_{1}=\{2,3\} then Di=∅subscript𝐷𝑖D_{i}=\emptyset for every i>1𝑖1i>1, and therefore

∑d∈D1d=∑d∈D01d+15​∑d∈D11d⩽16+15⋅56=13,subscript𝑑𝐷1𝑑subscript𝑑subscript𝐷01𝑑15subscript𝑑subscript𝐷11𝑑16⋅155613\sum_{d\in D}\frac{1}{d}\,=\,\sum_{d\in D_{0}}\frac{1}{d}\,+\frac{1}{5}\sum_{d\in D_{1}}\frac{1}{d}\,\leqslant\,\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{5}\cdot\frac{5}{6}\,=\,\frac{1}{3},

with equality only when D={6,10,15}𝐷61015D=\{6,10,15\}. On the other hand, if D1≠{2,3}subscript𝐷123D_{1}\neq\{2,3\} then

∑d∈D1d=∑i=0∞15i​∑d∈Di1d⩽16+115⋅18+∑i=2∞15i−1⋅6=119360<13,subscript𝑑𝐷1𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑖01superscript5𝑖subscript𝑑subscript𝐷𝑖1𝑑1611⋅518superscriptsubscript𝑖21⋅superscript5𝑖1611936013\sum_{d\in D}\frac{1}{d}\,=\,\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{5^{i}}\sum_{d\in D_{i}}\frac{1}{d}\,\leqslant\,\frac{1}{6}+\frac{11}{5\cdot 18}+\sum_{i=2}^{\infty}\frac{1}{5^{i-1}\cdot 6}\,=\,\frac{119}{360}\,<\,\frac{1}{3},

as required. ∎

Lemma 9.3.

If A𝐴A and B𝐵B are two 333-smooth antichains, then

∑a∈A∑b∈B1lcm⁡(a,b)⩽3136,subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1lcm𝑎𝑏3136\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)}\leqslant\,\frac{31}{36}, (28)

except in the cases A=B={1}𝐴𝐵1A=B=\{1\} and A=B={2,3}𝐴𝐵23A=B=\{2,3\}.

Note that the sum in (28) is equal to 111 if A=B={1}𝐴𝐵1A=B=\{1\}, and to 7/6767/6 if A=B={2,3}𝐴𝐵23A=B=\{2,3\}.

Proof.

Suppose first that |A|⩽|B|𝐴𝐵|A|\leqslant|B|, and that B={2k−1,2k−2​31,…,3k−1}𝐵superscript2𝑘1superscript2𝑘2superscript31…superscript3𝑘1B=\{2^{k-1},2^{k-2}3^{1},\dots,3^{k-1}\} for some k⩾5𝑘5k\geqslant 5. Then

∑a∈A∑b∈B1lcm⁡(a,b)⩽∑a∈A∑b∈B1b=|A|​∑b∈B1b= 6​k​(2−k−3−k)<3136.subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1lcm𝑎𝑏subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1𝑏𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1𝑏6𝑘superscript2𝑘superscript3𝑘3136\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)}\,\leqslant\,\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{b}\,=\,|A|\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{b}\,=\,6k\big{(}2^{-k}-3^{-k}\big{)}\,<\,\frac{31}{36}.

However, if |B|=k⩾5𝐵𝑘5|B|=k\geqslant 5 but B≠{2k−1,2k−2​31,…,3k−1}𝐵superscript2𝑘1superscript2𝑘2superscript31…superscript3𝑘1B\neq\{2^{k-1},2^{k-2}3^{1},\dots,3^{k-1}\}, then by compressing (as in the proof of Lemma 9.2) we may increase the left-hand side of (28), so we are also done in this case. The lemma therefore reduces to a finite check of families with max⁡{|A|,|B|}⩽4𝐴𝐵4\max\{|A|,|B|\}\leqslant 4, and in fact (using compression once again) it is sufficient to consider the antichains {1}1\{1\}, {2,3}23\{2,3\}, {2,9}29\{2,9\}, {3,4}34\{3,4\}, {4,6,9}469\{4,6,9\}, and {8,12,18,27}8121827\{8,12,18,27\}. The lemma now follows from a trivial case analysis, which can be done by hand. ∎

Lemma 9.4.

If A𝐴A and B𝐵B are two 555-smooth antichains, then

∑a∈A∑b∈B1lcm⁡(a,b)⩽ 1.7,subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1lcm𝑎𝑏1.7\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)}\leqslant\,1.7,

with equality if and only if A=B={2,3,5}𝐴𝐵235A=B=\{2,3,5\}.

Proof.

We decompose A𝐴A and B𝐵B as a union of sets 5i⋅Ai⋅superscript5𝑖subscript𝐴𝑖5^{i}\cdot A_{i} and 5j⋅Bj⋅superscript5𝑗subscript𝐵𝑗5^{j}\cdot B_{j}, where Aisubscript𝐴𝑖A_{i} and Bjsubscript𝐵𝑗B_{j} are 3-smooth antichains, as in the proof of Lemma 9.2. Suppose first that there is no pair (i,j)𝑖𝑗(i,j) with Ai=Bj={1}subscript𝐴𝑖subscript𝐵𝑗1A_{i}=B_{j}=\{1\} or Ai=Bj={2,3}subscript𝐴𝑖subscript𝐵𝑗23A_{i}=B_{j}=\{2,3\}. Then, by Lemma 9.3 and (17), we have

∑a∈A∑b∈B1lcm⁡(a,b)subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1lcm𝑎𝑏\displaystyle\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)} =∑i=0∞∑j=0∞15max⁡{i,j}​∑a∈Ai∑b∈Bj1lcm⁡(a,b)absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑖0superscriptsubscript𝑗01superscript5𝑖𝑗subscript𝑎subscript𝐴𝑖subscript𝑏subscript𝐵𝑗1lcm𝑎𝑏\displaystyle\,=\,\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{5^{\max\{i,j\}}}\sum_{a\in A_{i}}\sum_{b\in B_{j}}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)}
⩽3136​(1+35+552+753+⋯)=3136⋅158<1.7−112.absent31361355superscript527superscript53⋯⋅31361581.7112\displaystyle\,\leqslant\,\frac{31}{36}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3}{5}+\frac{5}{5^{2}}+\frac{7}{5^{3}}+\cdots\bigg{)}=\frac{31}{36}\cdot\frac{15}{8}<1.7-\frac{1}{12}.

Next, suppose that Ai=Bj={1}subscript𝐴𝑖subscript𝐵𝑗1A_{i}=B_{j}=\{1\} for some pair (i,j)𝑖𝑗(i,j), and observe that Ai′=Bj′=∅subscript𝐴superscript𝑖′subscript𝐵superscript𝑗′A_{i^{\prime}}=B_{j^{\prime}}=\emptyset for every i′>isuperscript𝑖′𝑖i^{\prime}>i and j′>jsuperscript𝑗′𝑗j^{\prime}>j, so the pair (i,j)𝑖𝑗(i,j) is unique. If there is no pair (s,t)𝑠𝑡(s,t) with As=Bt={2,3}subscript𝐴𝑠subscript𝐵𝑡23A_{s}=B_{t}=\{2,3\}, then the bound above increases by at most (1−3136)​5−max⁡{i,j}13136superscript5𝑖𝑗(1-\tfrac{31}{36})5^{-\max\{i,j\}}, and this is less that 112112\frac{1}{12} if max⁡{i,j}⩾1𝑖𝑗1\max\{i,j\}\geqslant 1. On the other hand, if A0=B0={1}subscript𝐴0subscript𝐵01A_{0}=B_{0}=\{1\}, then (since Ai=Bi=∅subscript𝐴𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖A_{i}=B_{i}=\emptyset for all i>0𝑖0i>0) we have A=B={1}𝐴𝐵1A=B=\{1\}, and so ∑a∈A∑b∈B1lcm⁡(a,b)=1<1.7subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1lcm𝑎𝑏11.7\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)}=1<1.7.

We may therefore assume that Ai=Bj={2,3}subscript𝐴𝑖subscript𝐵𝑗23A_{i}=B_{j}=\{2,3\} for some pair (i,j)𝑖𝑗(i,j), which implies that Ai′,Bj′⊆{1}subscript𝐴superscript𝑖′subscript𝐵superscript𝑗′1A_{i^{\prime}},B_{j^{\prime}}\subseteq\{1\} for every i′>isuperscript𝑖′𝑖i^{\prime}>i and j′>jsuperscript𝑗′𝑗j^{\prime}>j, and (as above) at most one of the sets in each sequence is non-empty. The bound above increases by at most

(76−3136)​15max⁡{i,j}+(1−3136)​15max⁡{i,j}+1=13⋅15max⁡{i,j}<1127631361superscript5𝑖𝑗131361superscript5𝑖𝑗1⋅131superscript5𝑖𝑗112\bigg{(}\frac{7}{6}-\frac{31}{36}\bigg{)}\frac{1}{5^{\max\{i,j\}}}+\bigg{(}1-\frac{31}{36}\bigg{)}\frac{1}{5^{\max\{i,j\}+1}}=\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{1}{5^{\max\{i,j\}}}<\frac{1}{12}

if max⁡{i,j}⩾1𝑖𝑗1\max\{i,j\}\geqslant 1. However, if A0=B0={2,3}subscript𝐴0subscript𝐵023A_{0}=B_{0}=\{2,3\}, then it is easy to see that ∑a∈A∑b∈B1lcm⁡(a,b)subscript𝑎𝐴subscript𝑏𝐵1lcm𝑎𝑏\sum_{a\in A}\sum_{b\in B}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)} is maximized by taking A=B={2,3,5}𝐴𝐵235A=B=\{2,3,5\}, and in that case it is equal to 1.71.71.7. ∎

Having completed the easy preliminaries, we are ready to prove Schinzel’s conjecture.

Proof of Theorem 1.2..

We first observe that we may assume that none of the moduli disubscript𝑑𝑖d_{i} are prime powers. Indeed, we may assume that the covering is minimal, so the removal of any Adisubscript𝐴subscript𝑑𝑖A_{d_{i}} results in a set of progressions that do not cover ℤℤ\mathbb{Z}. If di=pjsubscript𝑑𝑖superscript𝑝𝑗d_{i}=p^{j} for some prime p𝑝p and j>0𝑗0j>0, then the prime can appear at most to the (j−1)𝑗1(j-1)st power in any other moduli. Thus the other progressions fail to cover some congruence class mod Q/p𝑄𝑝Q/p, where Q=lcm⁡{d1,…,dk}𝑄lcmsubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘Q=\operatorname{lcm}\{d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\}. But this congruence class cannot be covered by Adisubscript𝐴subscript𝑑𝑖A_{d_{i}} as di∤Q/pnot-dividessubscript𝑑𝑖𝑄𝑝d_{i}\nmid Q/p, a contradiction.

We now apply our sieve with δ1=δ2=δ3=0subscript𝛿1subscript𝛿2subscript𝛿30\delta_{1}=\delta_{2}=\delta_{3}=0, so that ℙisubscriptℙ𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{i} is equal to the uniform measure when processing the primes p1=2subscript𝑝12p_{1}=2, p2=3subscript𝑝23p_{2}=3 and p3=5subscript𝑝35p_{3}=5, and claim that f3⩽2.55<g3subscript𝑓32.55subscript𝑔3f_{3}\leqslant 2.55<g_{3}. Observe that, by Lemma 9.2, the total measure of B1∪B2∪B3subscript𝐵1subscript𝐵2subscript𝐵3B_{1}\cup B_{2}\cup B_{3} is at most 1/3131/3. Now we improve the bound on 𝔼⁡[αi​(x)2]𝔼subscript𝛼𝑖superscript𝑥2\operatorname{\mathbb{E}}[\alpha_{i}(x)^{2}] for i⩾4𝑖4i\geqslant 4 as follows. By Theorem 3.2, we have

Mi(2)⩽∑j1,j2⩾11pij1+j2​∑m1,m2∈Siν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)​∑a∈D​(m1,j1)∑b∈D​(m2,j2)1lcm⁡(a,b),superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖2subscriptsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗211superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2subscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑆𝑖𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑎𝐷subscript𝑚1subscript𝑗1subscript𝑏𝐷subscript𝑚2subscript𝑗21lcm𝑎𝑏M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant\sum_{j_{1},j_{2}\geqslant 1}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+j_{2}}}\sum_{m_{1},m_{2}\in S_{i}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}\sum_{a\in D(m_{1},j_{1})}\sum_{b\in D(m_{2},j_{2})}\frac{1}{\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)},

where Sisubscript𝑆𝑖S_{i} is the set of integers whose prime factors all lie between 777 and pi−1subscript𝑝𝑖1p_{i-1}, and for each m∈Si𝑚subscript𝑆𝑖m\in S_{i} and j⩾1𝑗1j\geqslant 1, we define

D​(m,j)={a:m​pij​a∈D​ and a is 5-smooth}.𝐷𝑚𝑗conditional-set𝑎𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖𝑗𝑎𝐷 and a is 5-smoothD(m,j)=\big{\{}a:mp_{i}^{j}a\in D\text{ and $a$ is 5-smooth}\big{\}}.

Since D​(m,j)𝐷𝑚𝑗D(m,j) is a 5-smooth antichain, it follows from Lemmas 3.7 and 9.4 that

Mi(2)⩽1.7​∑j1,j2⩾11pij1+j2​∑m1,m2∈Siν​(lcm⁡(m1,m2))lcm⁡(m1,m2)=1.7(pi−1)2​∏j=4i−1(1+3​pj−1(1−δj)​(pj−1)2).superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖21.7subscriptsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗211superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2subscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑆𝑖𝜈lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2lcmsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚21.7superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑖12superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑗4𝑖113subscript𝑝𝑗11subscript𝛿𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑗12M_{i}^{(2)}\leqslant 1.7\sum_{j_{1},j_{2}\geqslant 1}\frac{1}{p_{i}^{j_{1}+j_{2}}}\sum_{m_{1},m_{2}\in S_{i}}\frac{\nu\big{(}\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})\big{)}}{\operatorname{lcm}(m_{1},m_{2})}\,=\,\frac{1.7}{(p_{i}-1)^{2}}\prod_{j=4}^{i-1}\bigg{(}1+\frac{3p_{j}-1}{(1-\delta_{j})(p_{j}-1)^{2}}\bigg{)}.

Therefore, setting i0=3subscript𝑖03i_{0}=3 and κ=1.7𝜅1.7\kappa=1.7, it follows that (20) holds. Hence, recalling from above that μ3⩾2/3subscript𝜇323\mu_{3}\geqslant 2/3, we obtain f3=1.7⋅3/2=2.55<g3subscript𝑓3⋅1.7322.55subscript𝑔3f_{3}=1.7\cdot 3/2=2.55<g_{3} (see Table 1), so, by Corollary 6.3, the system 𝒜={Ad:d∈D}𝒜conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑𝐷\mathcal{A}=\{A_{d}:d\in D\} does not cover the integers. ∎

10. Constructions

In this section we will provide constructions of families of arithmetic progressions that answer (negatively) the question of Filaseta, Ford, Konyagin, Pomerance and Yu [7] mentioned in the Introduction, and show that Theorem 1.1 is not far from best possible. To be precise, we will prove the following two theorems.

Theorem 10.1.

For every M>0𝑀0M>0 and ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0, there exists a finite collection of arithmetic progressions A1,…,Aksubscript𝐴1…subscript𝐴𝑘A_{1},\dots,A_{k} with distinct moduli d1,…,dk⩾Msubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘𝑀d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\geqslant M, such that

∑i=1k1di<1superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘1subscript𝑑𝑖1\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{1}{d_{i}}<1 (29)

and the density of the uncovered set R=ℤ∖⋃i=1kAi𝑅ℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘subscript𝐴𝑖R=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}A_{i} is less than ε𝜀\varepsilon.

Note that the bound (29) is (obviously) best possible; we remark that the moduli in our construction will moreover be square-free. Our second theorem shows that the function (log⁡p)3+εsuperscript𝑝3𝜀(\log p)^{3+\varepsilon} in the statement of Theorem 1.1 cannot be replaced by a constant.

Theorem 10.2.

For every λ>0𝜆0\lambda>0, there exists C=C​(λ)>0𝐶𝐶𝜆0C=C(\lambda)>0 such that the following holds. Let μ𝜇\mu be the multiplicative function defined by

μ​(pi)=1+λp𝜇superscript𝑝𝑖1𝜆𝑝\mu(p^{i})=1+\frac{\lambda}{p}

for all primes p𝑝p and integers i⩾1𝑖1i\geqslant 1. For every M>0𝑀0M>0 and ε>0𝜀0\varepsilon>0, there exists a finite collection of arithmetic progressions A1,…,Aksubscript𝐴1…subscript𝐴𝑘A_{1},\dots,A_{k} with distinct square-free moduli d1,…,dk⩾Msubscript𝑑1…subscript𝑑𝑘𝑀d_{1},\dots,d_{k}\geqslant M, such that

∑i=1kμ​(di)di⩽C,superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘𝜇subscript𝑑𝑖subscript𝑑𝑖𝐶\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{\mu(d_{i})}{d_{i}}\leqslant C,

and the density of the uncovered set R=ℤ∖⋃i=1kAi𝑅ℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑘subscript𝐴𝑖R=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}A_{i} is at most ε𝜀\varepsilon.

The proof of Theorem 10.1 is relatively simple, while the proof of Theorem 10.2 will require somewhat more work.

Proof of Theorem 10.1.

We will choose a collection P1,…,PNsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝑁P_{1},\dots,P_{N} of disjoint sets of primes, and define

Qi:=∏j⩽i∏p∈PjpandDi:={p⋅Qi−1:p∈Pi}formulae-sequenceassignsubscript𝑄𝑖subscriptproduct𝑗𝑖subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃𝑗𝑝andassignsubscript𝐷𝑖conditional-set⋅𝑝subscript𝑄𝑖1𝑝subscript𝑃𝑖Q_{i}:=\prod_{j\leqslant i}\prod_{p\in P_{j}}p\qquad\text{and}\qquad D_{i}:=\big{\{}p\cdot Q_{i-1}:p\in P_{i}\big{\}}

for each i∈[N]𝑖delimited-[]𝑁i\in[N], where Q0:=1assignsubscript𝑄01Q_{0}:=1. We will show that, for a suitable choice of P1,…,PNsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝑁P_{1},\dots,P_{N}, the set D=D1∪⋯∪DN𝐷subscript𝐷1⋯subscript𝐷𝑁D=D_{1}\cup\cdots\cup D_{N} has the following properties:

∑d∈D1d⩽1+ε3,subscript𝑑𝐷1𝑑1𝜀3\sum_{d\in D}\frac{1}{d}\leqslant 1+\frac{\varepsilon}{3}, (30)

and there exists a collection of arithmetic progressions, with distinct moduli in D𝐷D, such that the uncovered set has density at most ε/3𝜀3\varepsilon/3. By removing a few of the progressions from this family, we will obtain the claimed collection A1,…,Aksubscript𝐴1…subscript𝐴𝑘A_{1},\dots,A_{k}.

We construct the sets P1,…,PNsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝑁P_{1},\dots,P_{N} of primes as follows. First, let us fix some positive constants c0subscript𝑐0c_{0}, c𝑐c and δ𝛿\delta such that

c0:=1+ε3,andc=δ1−e−δ∈(1,c0).formulae-sequenceassignsubscript𝑐01𝜀3and𝑐𝛿1superscript𝑒𝛿1subscript𝑐0c_{0}:=1+\frac{\varepsilon}{3},\qquad\text{and}\qquad c=\frac{\delta}{1-e^{-\delta}}\in(1,c_{0}).

Indeed, δ/(1−e−δ)𝛿1superscript𝑒𝛿\delta/(1-e^{-\delta}) is a continuous increasing function of δ𝛿\delta which tends to 1 as δ→0→𝛿0\delta\rightarrow 0, so for sufficiently small δ𝛿\delta we have 1<δ/(1−e−δ)<c01𝛿1superscript𝑒𝛿subscript𝑐01<\delta/(1-e^{-\delta})<c_{0}. Assume (without loss) that M>3/ε𝑀3𝜀M>3/\varepsilon, and choose N𝑁N sufficiently large so that e−δ​N<ε/3superscript𝑒𝛿𝑁𝜀3e^{-\delta N}<\varepsilon/3. Now let P1subscript𝑃1P_{1} be any set of primes such that p⩾M𝑝𝑀p\geqslant M for every p∈P1𝑝subscript𝑃1p\in P_{1}, and

δ⩽∑p∈P11p⩽δ+c0−cN.𝛿subscript𝑝subscript𝑃11𝑝𝛿subscript𝑐0𝑐𝑁\delta\leqslant\sum_{p\in P_{1}}\frac{1}{p}\leqslant\delta+\frac{c_{0}-c}{N}.

In general, if we have already constructed P1,…,Pjsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝑗P_{1},\dots,P_{j}, then let Pj+1subscript𝑃𝑗1P_{j+1} be any set of primes (disjoint from P1∪⋯∪Pjsubscript𝑃1⋯subscript𝑃𝑗P_{1}\cup\cdots\cup P_{j}) such that p⩾M𝑝𝑀p\geqslant M for every p∈Pj+1𝑝subscript𝑃𝑗1p\in P_{j+1}, and

δ​e−δ​j⩽∑p∈Pj+11p⋅Qj⩽δ​e−δ​j+c0−cN.𝛿superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗subscript𝑝subscript𝑃𝑗11⋅𝑝subscript𝑄𝑗𝛿superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗subscript𝑐0𝑐𝑁\delta e^{-\delta j}\leqslant\sum_{p\in P_{j+1}}\frac{1}{p\cdot Q_{j}}\leqslant\delta e^{-\delta j}+\frac{c_{0}-c}{N}. (31)

As the sum ∑1/p1𝑝\sum 1/p over prime p𝑝p diverges, it is clear that sets P1,…,PNsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝑁P_{1},\dots,P_{N} exist with these properties. It follows that

∑d∈D1d=∑j=0N−1∑p∈Pj+11p⋅Qj⩽∑j=0N−1(δ​e−δ​j+c0−cN)⩽c0=1+ε3,subscript𝑑𝐷1𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑗0𝑁1subscript𝑝subscript𝑃𝑗11⋅𝑝subscript𝑄𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑗0𝑁1𝛿superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗subscript𝑐0𝑐𝑁subscript𝑐01𝜀3\sum_{d\in D}\frac{1}{d}=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\sum_{p\in P_{j+1}}\frac{1}{p\cdot Q_{j}}\leqslant\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\bigg{(}\delta e^{-\delta j}+\frac{c_{0}-c}{N}\bigg{)}\leqslant c_{0}=1+\frac{\varepsilon}{3},

where in the final inequality we used the identity ∑j=0∞δ​e−δ​j=δ​(1−e−δ)−1=csuperscriptsubscript𝑗0𝛿superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗𝛿superscript1superscript𝑒𝛿1𝑐\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\delta e^{-\delta j}=\delta\big{(}1-e^{-\delta}\big{)}^{-1}=c.

Now, to construct the arithmetic progressions, simply choose (for each d∈D𝑑𝐷d\in D in turn) any arithmetic progression with modulus d𝑑d that has at least the expected intersection with the (as yet) uncovered set. To be more precise, for each j∈[N]𝑗delimited-[]𝑁j\in[N] let 𝒜jsubscript𝒜𝑗\mathcal{A}_{j} denote the collection of arithmetic progressions whose modulus lies in Djsubscript𝐷𝑗D_{j}, and write εj:=ℙ0⁡(Rj)assignsubscript𝜀𝑗subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅𝑗\varepsilon_{j}:=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{j}) for the density of the uncovered set Rj:=ℤQj∖⋃A∈ℬjAassignsubscript𝑅𝑗subscriptℤsubscript𝑄𝑗subscript𝐴subscriptℬ𝑗𝐴R_{j}:=\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{j}}\setminus\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{B}_{j}}A, where ℬj:=𝒜1∪⋯∪𝒜jassignsubscriptℬ𝑗subscript𝒜1⋯subscript𝒜𝑗\mathcal{B}_{j}:=\mathcal{A}_{1}\cup\dots\cup\mathcal{A}_{j}. Now observe that if d=p⋅Qj∈Dj+1𝑑⋅𝑝subscript𝑄𝑗subscript𝐷𝑗1d=p\cdot Q_{j}\in D_{j+1}, then there are p⋅|Rj|⋅𝑝subscript𝑅𝑗p\cdot|R_{j}| congruence classes mod d𝑑d that completely cover Rjsubscript𝑅𝑗R_{j}. Hence there is a congruence class that covers at least a fraction 1/(p⋅|Rj|)1⋅𝑝subscript𝑅𝑗1/(p\cdot|R_{j}|) of the as yet uncovered set. It follows that

εj+1⩽εj​∏p∈Pj+1(1−1p⋅|Rj|)⩽εj⋅exp⁡(−1|Rj|​∑p∈Pj+11p)subscript𝜀𝑗1subscript𝜀𝑗subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃𝑗111⋅𝑝subscript𝑅𝑗⋅subscript𝜀𝑗1subscript𝑅𝑗subscript𝑝subscript𝑃𝑗11𝑝\varepsilon_{j+1}\leqslant\varepsilon_{j}\prod_{p\in P_{j+1}}\bigg{(}1-\frac{1}{p\cdot|R_{j}|}\bigg{)}\leqslant\varepsilon_{j}\cdot\exp\bigg{(}-\frac{1}{|R_{j}|}\sum_{p\in P_{j+1}}\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}

for each 0⩽j⩽N−10𝑗𝑁10\leqslant j\leqslant N-1 (where ε0:=1assignsubscript𝜀01\varepsilon_{0}:=1), and hence, by (31),

εj+1⩽εj⋅exp⁡(−Qj|Rj|⋅δ​e−δ​j)=εj⋅exp⁡(−δ​e−δ​jεj).subscript𝜀𝑗1⋅subscript𝜀𝑗⋅subscript𝑄𝑗subscript𝑅𝑗𝛿superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗⋅subscript𝜀𝑗𝛿superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗subscript𝜀𝑗\varepsilon_{j+1}\leqslant\varepsilon_{j}\cdot\exp\bigg{(}-\frac{Q_{j}}{|R_{j}|}\cdot\delta e^{-\delta j}\bigg{)}=\varepsilon_{j}\cdot\exp\bigg{(}-\frac{\delta e^{-\delta j}}{\varepsilon_{j}}\bigg{)}.

It now follows immediately by induction that εj⩽e−δ​jsubscript𝜀𝑗superscript𝑒𝛿𝑗\varepsilon_{j}\leqslant e^{-\delta j} for every j∈[N]𝑗delimited-[]𝑁j\in[N], and in particular εN⩽e−δ​N⩽ε/3subscript𝜀𝑁superscript𝑒𝛿𝑁𝜀3\varepsilon_{N}\leqslant e^{-\delta N}\leqslant\varepsilon/3, by our choice of N𝑁N.

We have therefore constructed a collection of arithmetic progressions whose set D𝐷D of (distinct) moduli satisfies (30), and whose uncovered set has density at most ε/3𝜀3\varepsilon/3. To complete the construction, simply choose a maximal subset D′⊂Dsuperscript𝐷′𝐷D^{\prime}\subset D such that ∑d∈D′1d<1subscript𝑑superscript𝐷′1𝑑1\sum_{d\in D^{\prime}}\frac{1}{d}<1, and observe that the density of the set uncovered by {Ad:d∈D′}conditional-setsubscript𝐴𝑑𝑑superscript𝐷′\big{\{}A_{d}:d\in D^{\prime}\big{\}} is at most

ε3+∑d∈D∖D′1d⩽ε3+ε3+1M⩽ε,𝜀3subscript𝑑𝐷superscript𝐷′1𝑑𝜀3𝜀31𝑀𝜀\frac{\varepsilon}{3}+\sum_{d\in D\setminus D^{\prime}}\frac{1}{d}\leqslant\frac{\varepsilon}{3}+\frac{\varepsilon}{3}+\frac{1}{M}\leqslant\varepsilon,

as required. ∎

The proof of Theorem 10.2 is similar to that of Theorem 10.1, but the details are somewhat more complicated.

Proof of Theorem 10.2.

We will again choose a large collection of disjoint sets of primes, but this time we will arrange them in a tree-like structure, and our common differences will be formed by taking products of certain subsets of the primes along paths in the tree. To begin, let us choose t>2𝑡2t>2 sufficiently large so that tλ<et−3superscript𝑡𝜆superscript𝑒𝑡3t^{\lambda}<e^{t-3}, and let P1subscript𝑃1P_{1} be a set of primes such that p⩾M𝑝𝑀p\geqslant M for every p∈P1𝑝subscript𝑃1p\in P_{1}, and

t−1⩽∏p∈P1(1+1p)⩽t.𝑡1subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃111𝑝𝑡t-1\leqslant\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}\leqslant t.

This is possible as the product ∏p(1+1/p)subscriptproduct𝑝11𝑝\prod_{p}(1+1/p) over all primes is infinite, and by taking only primes greater than t𝑡t we can ensure that some finite product lands in [t−1,t]𝑡1𝑡[t-1,t]. Set

Q1:=∏p∈P1pandD1:={d>1:d∣Q1},formulae-sequenceassignsubscript𝑄1subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃1𝑝andassignsubscript𝐷1conditional-set𝑑1conditional𝑑subscript𝑄1Q_{1}:=\prod_{p\in P_{1}}p\qquad\text{and}\qquad D_{1}:=\big{\{}d>1:d\mid Q_{1}\big{\}},

and choose a collection of arithmetic progressions 𝒜1={ad+d​ℤ:d∈D1}subscript𝒜1conditional-setsubscript𝑎𝑑𝑑ℤ𝑑subscript𝐷1\mathcal{A}_{1}=\{a_{d}+d\mathbb{Z}:d\in D_{1}\} so as to minimize the density of the uncovered set R1:=ℤQ1∖⋃A∈𝒜1Aassignsubscript𝑅1subscriptℤsubscript𝑄1subscript𝐴subscript𝒜1𝐴R_{1}:=\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{1}}\setminus\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}_{1}}A. As in the previous proof, this can be done (greedily) so that

ℙ0⁡(R1)⩽∏d∈D1(1−1d)⩽exp⁡(−∑d∈D11d)⩽exp⁡(1−∏p∈P1(1+1p))⩽e2−t.subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅1subscriptproduct𝑑subscript𝐷111𝑑subscript𝑑subscript𝐷11𝑑1subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃111𝑝superscript𝑒2𝑡\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{1})\leqslant\prod_{d\in D_{1}}\bigg{(}1-\frac{1}{d}\bigg{)}\leqslant\exp\bigg{(}-\sum_{d\in D_{1}}\frac{1}{d}\bigg{)}\leqslant\exp\bigg{(}1-\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}\bigg{)}\leqslant e^{2-t}.

Now, for each x1∈R1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1x_{1}\in R_{1}, let P2x1superscriptsubscript𝑃2subscript𝑥1P_{2}^{x_{1}} be a set of new primes (i.e., disjoint for each choice of x1subscript𝑥1x_{1}, and disjoint from P1subscript𝑃1P_{1}) such that p⩾M𝑝𝑀p\geqslant M for every p∈P2x1𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃2subscript𝑥1p\in P_{2}^{x_{1}}, and

t−1⩽∏p∈P2x1(1+1p)⩽t.𝑡1subscriptproduct𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃2subscript𝑥111𝑝𝑡t-1\leqslant\prod_{p\in P_{2}^{x_{1}}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}\leqslant t.

Set

Q2x1:=∏p∈P2x1pandD2x1:={d⋅Q1:d>1​ and ​d∣Q2x1},formulae-sequenceassignsuperscriptsubscript𝑄2subscript𝑥1subscriptproduct𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃2subscript𝑥1𝑝andassignsuperscriptsubscript𝐷2subscript𝑥1conditional-set⋅𝑑subscript𝑄1𝑑conditional1 and 𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑄2subscript𝑥1Q_{2}^{x_{1}}:=\prod_{p\in P_{2}^{x_{1}}}p\qquad\text{and}\qquad D_{2}^{x_{1}}:=\big{\{}d\cdot Q_{1}:d>1\text{ and }d\mid Q_{2}^{x_{1}}\big{\}},

and choose a collection of arithmetic progressions 𝒜2x1={ad+d​ℤ:d∈D2x1}superscriptsubscript𝒜2subscript𝑥1conditional-setsubscript𝑎𝑑𝑑ℤ𝑑superscriptsubscript𝐷2subscript𝑥1\mathcal{A}_{2}^{x_{1}}=\{a_{d}+d\mathbb{Z}:d\in D_{2}^{x_{1}}\} so as to minimize the density of the uncovered set

R2x1:={(x1,y):y∈ℤQ2x1}∖⋃A∈𝒜2x1A,assignsuperscriptsubscript𝑅2subscript𝑥1conditional-setsubscript𝑥1𝑦𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑄2subscript𝑥1subscript𝐴superscriptsubscript𝒜2subscript𝑥1𝐴R_{2}^{x_{1}}:=\Big{\{}(x_{1},y):y\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{2}^{x_{1}}}\Big{\}}\setminus\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}_{2}^{x_{1}}}A,

where (as usual) each A∈𝒜2x1𝐴superscriptsubscript𝒜2subscript𝑥1A\in\mathcal{A}_{2}^{x_{1}} is viewed as a subset of ℤQ1×ℤQ2x1subscriptℤsubscript𝑄1subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑄2subscript𝑥1\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{1}}\times\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{2}^{x_{1}}}. Note that

ℙ0⁡(R2x1)⩽1Q1​∏d​Q1∈D2x1(1−1d)⩽1Q1⋅exp⁡(1−∏p∈P2x1(1+1p))⩽e2−tQ1,subscriptℙ0superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscript𝑥11subscript𝑄1subscriptproduct𝑑subscript𝑄1superscriptsubscript𝐷2subscript𝑥111𝑑⋅1subscript𝑄11subscriptproduct𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃2subscript𝑥111𝑝superscript𝑒2𝑡subscript𝑄1\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}\big{(}R_{2}^{x_{1}}\big{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{Q_{1}}\prod_{dQ_{1}\in D_{2}^{x_{1}}}\bigg{(}1-\frac{1}{d}\bigg{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{Q_{1}}\cdot\exp\bigg{(}1-\prod_{p\in P_{2}^{x_{1}}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}\bigg{)}\leqslant\frac{e^{2-t}}{Q_{1}},

and hence, setting R2:=⋃x1∈R1R2x1assignsubscript𝑅2subscriptsubscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscript𝑥1R_{2}:=\bigcup_{x_{1}\in R_{1}}R_{2}^{x_{1}} and summing over x1∈R1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1x_{1}\in R_{1}, we have

ℙ0⁡(R2)⩽|R1|⋅e2−tQ1=ℙ0⁡(R1)⋅e2−t⩽e2​(2−t).subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅2⋅subscript𝑅1superscript𝑒2𝑡subscript𝑄1⋅subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅1superscript𝑒2𝑡superscript𝑒22𝑡\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{2})\leqslant|R_{1}|\cdot\frac{e^{2-t}}{Q_{1}}=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{1})\cdot e^{2-t}\leqslant e^{2(2-t)}.

To describe a general step of this construction, suppose that we have already defined the tree of primes and progressions to depth i−1𝑖1i-1, and for each x1∈R1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1x_{1}\in R_{1}, x2∈R2x2subscript𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝑅2subscript𝑥2x_{2}\in R_{2}^{x_{2}}, …, xi−1∈Ri−1x1,…,xi−2subscript𝑥𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2x_{i-1}\in R_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}, choose a set Pix1,…,xi−1superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1P_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}} of new primes (disjoint from all previously chosen sets) such that p⩾M𝑝𝑀p\geqslant M for every p∈Pix1,…,xi−1𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1p\in P_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}, and

t−1⩽∏p∈Pix1,…,xi−1(1+1p)⩽t.𝑡1subscriptproduct𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖111𝑝𝑡t-1\leqslant\prod_{p\in P_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}\leqslant t.

Set

Qix1,…,xi−1:=∏p∈Pix1,…,xi−1passignsuperscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1subscriptproduct𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1𝑝Q_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}:=\prod_{p\in P_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}p

and

Dix1,…,xi−1:={d⋅Q1⋅Q2x1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2:d>1​ and ​d∣Qix1,…,xi−1},assignsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1conditional-set⋅𝑑subscript𝑄1superscriptsubscript𝑄2subscript𝑥1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2𝑑conditional1 and 𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1D_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}:=\big{\{}d\cdot Q_{1}\cdot Q_{2}^{x_{1}}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}:d>1\text{ and }d\mid Q_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}\big{\}},

and choose a collection of arithmetic progressions 𝒜ix1,…,xi−1={ad+d​ℤ:d∈Dix1,…,xi−1}superscriptsubscript𝒜𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1conditional-setsubscript𝑎𝑑𝑑ℤ𝑑superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1\mathcal{A}_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}=\{a_{d}+d\mathbb{Z}:d\in D_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}\} so as to minimize the density of the uncovered set

Rix1,…,xi−1:={(xi−1,y):y∈ℤQix1,…,xi−1}∖⋃A∈𝒜ix1,…,xi−1A,assignsuperscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1conditional-setsubscript𝑥𝑖1𝑦𝑦subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1subscript𝐴superscriptsubscript𝒜𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1𝐴R_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}:=\Big{\{}(x_{i-1},y):y\in\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}\Big{\}}\setminus\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}A,

where, as before, each A∈𝒜ix1,…,xi−1𝐴superscriptsubscript𝒜𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1A\in\mathcal{A}_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}} is viewed as a subset of ℤQ1×⋯×ℤQix1,…,xi−1subscriptℤsubscript𝑄1⋯subscriptℤsuperscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{1}}\times\cdots\times\mathbb{Z}_{Q_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}. Setting Ri:=⋃x1∈R1⋯​⋃xi−1∈Ri−1x1,…,xi−2Rix1,…,xi−1assignsubscript𝑅𝑖subscriptsubscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1⋯subscriptsubscript𝑥𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1R_{i}:=\bigcup_{x_{1}\in R_{1}}\cdots\bigcup_{x_{i-1}\in R_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}}R_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}, and repeating the calculation above, we obtain

ℙ0⁡(Rix1,…,xi−1)⩽1Q1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2​∏d​Q1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2∈Dix1,…,xi−1(1−1d)⩽e2−tQ1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2,subscriptℙ0superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖11subscript𝑄1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2subscriptproduct𝑑subscript𝑄1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖111𝑑superscript𝑒2𝑡subscript𝑄1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}\big{(}R_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}\big{)}\leqslant\frac{1}{Q_{1}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}}\prod_{dQ_{1}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}\in D_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}\bigg{(}1-\frac{1}{d}\bigg{)}\leqslant\frac{e^{2-t}}{Q_{1}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}},

and therefore

ℙ0⁡(Ri)⩽ℙ0⁡(Ri−1)⋅e2−t⩽e(2−t)​i,subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅𝑖⋅subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅𝑖1superscript𝑒2𝑡superscript𝑒2𝑡𝑖\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{i})\leqslant\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{i-1})\cdot e^{2-t}\leqslant e^{(2-t)i},

by induction. Hence, defining 𝒜isubscript𝒜𝑖\mathcal{A}_{i} to be the union of all 𝒜ix1,…,xi−1superscriptsubscript𝒜𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1\mathcal{A}_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}} and 𝒜=𝒜1∪⋯∪𝒜n𝒜subscript𝒜1⋯subscript𝒜𝑛\mathcal{A}=\mathcal{A}_{1}\cup\cdots\cup\mathcal{A}_{n}, it follows that the uncovered set R𝑅R has density

ℙ0⁡(R)=ℙ0⁡(Rn)⩽e(2−t)​n→0subscriptℙ0𝑅subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅𝑛superscript𝑒2𝑡𝑛→0\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R)=\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{n})\leqslant e^{(2-t)n}\rightarrow 0

as n→∞→𝑛n\rightarrow\infty.

It remains to show that

∑d∈Dμ​(d)d⩽Csubscript𝑑𝐷𝜇𝑑𝑑𝐶\sum_{d\in D}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant C

for every n∈ℕ𝑛ℕn\in\mathbb{N}, where D𝐷D is the set of moduli of progressions in 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A}. To prove this, observe first that

μ​(Q1)=∏p∈P1(1+λp)⩽∏p∈P1(1+1p)λ⩽tλ,𝜇subscript𝑄1subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃11𝜆𝑝subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃1superscript11𝑝𝜆superscript𝑡𝜆\mu(Q_{1})=\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{\lambda}{p}\bigg{)}\leqslant\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}^{\lambda}\leqslant t^{\lambda},

and, assuming (as we may) that M⩾λ𝑀𝜆M\geqslant\lambda,

∑d∈D1μ​(d)d=∏p∈P1(1+μ​(p)p)−1⩽∏p∈P1(1+1p+λp2)⩽∏p∈P1(1+1p)2⩽t2.subscript𝑑subscript𝐷1𝜇𝑑𝑑subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃11𝜇𝑝𝑝1subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃111𝑝𝜆superscript𝑝2subscriptproduct𝑝subscript𝑃1superscript11𝑝2superscript𝑡2\sum_{d\in D_{1}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}=\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{\mu(p)}{p}\bigg{)}-1\leqslant\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}+\frac{\lambda}{p^{2}}\bigg{)}\leqslant\prod_{p\in P_{1}}\bigg{(}1+\frac{1}{p}\bigg{)}^{2}\leqslant t^{2}.

Similarly, we have

∑d∈D2x1μ​(d)d=μ​(Q1)Q1​∑d​Q1∈D2x1μ​(d)d⩽tλ+2Q1subscript𝑑superscriptsubscript𝐷2subscript𝑥1𝜇𝑑𝑑𝜇subscript𝑄1subscript𝑄1subscript𝑑subscript𝑄1superscriptsubscript𝐷2subscript𝑥1𝜇𝑑𝑑superscript𝑡𝜆2subscript𝑄1\sum_{d\in D_{2}^{x_{1}}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}=\frac{\mu(Q_{1})}{Q_{1}}\sum_{dQ_{1}\in D_{2}^{x_{1}}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\frac{t^{\lambda+2}}{Q_{1}}

for each x1∈R1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1x_{1}\in R_{1}, and, more generally,

∑d∈Dix1,…,xi−1μ​(d)d⩽μ​(Q1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2)Q1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2​∑d∣Qix1,…,xi−1μ​(d)d⩽tλ​(i−1)+2Q1​⋯​Qi−1x1,…,xi−2.subscript𝑑superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1𝜇𝑑𝑑𝜇subscript𝑄1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2subscript𝑄1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2subscriptconditional𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖1𝜇𝑑𝑑superscript𝑡𝜆𝑖12subscript𝑄1⋯superscriptsubscript𝑄𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2\sum_{d\in D_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\frac{\mu\big{(}Q_{1}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}\big{)}}{Q_{1}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}}\sum_{d\mid Q_{i}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}}}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\frac{t^{\lambda(i-1)+2}}{Q_{1}\cdots Q_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}}.

Hence, summing over i∈[n]𝑖delimited-[]𝑛i\in[n] and sequences x1∈R1,…,xi−1∈Ri−1x1,…,xi−2formulae-sequencesubscript𝑥1subscript𝑅1…subscript𝑥𝑖1superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑖1subscript𝑥1…subscript𝑥𝑖2x_{1}\in R_{1},\dots,x_{i-1}\in R_{i-1}^{x_{1},\dots,x_{i-2}}, we obtain

∑d∈Dμ​(d)d⩽∑i=0n−1tλ​i+2⋅ℙ0⁡(Ri)⩽∑i=0n−1tλ​i+2⋅e(2−t)​i<2​t2,subscript𝑑𝐷𝜇𝑑𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑖0𝑛1⋅superscript𝑡𝜆𝑖2subscriptℙ0subscript𝑅𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑖0𝑛1⋅superscript𝑡𝜆𝑖2superscript𝑒2𝑡𝑖2superscript𝑡2\sum_{d\in D}\frac{\mu(d)}{d}\leqslant\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}t^{\lambda i+2}\cdot\operatorname{\mathbb{P}}_{0}(R_{i})\leqslant\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}t^{\lambda i+2}\cdot e^{(2-t)i}<2t^{2},

since tλ<et−3superscript𝑡𝜆superscript𝑒𝑡3t^{\lambda}<e^{t-3}, as required. ∎

Acknowledgements

This research was largely carried out during a one-month visit by the authors to IMT Lucca, and partly during visits by various subsets of the authors to IMPA and to the University of Memphis. We are grateful to each of these institutions for their hospitality, and for providing a wonderful working environment.

References

  • [1] P. Balister, B. Bollobás, R. Morris, J. Sahasrabudhe and M. Tiba, The Erdős–Selfridge problem with square-free moduli, in preparation.
  • [2] P. Dusart, The k𝑘kth prime is greater than k​(log⁡k+log⁡log⁡k−1)𝑘𝑘𝑘1k(\log k+\log\log k-1) for k⩾2𝑘2k\geqslant 2, Math. Comput., 68 (1999), 411–415.
  • [3] P. Erdős, On integers of the form 2k+psuperscript2𝑘𝑝2^{k}+p and some related problems, Summa Brasil. Math., 2 (1950), 113–123.
  • [4] P. Erdős, Problems and results in combinatorial number theory, A survey of combinatorial theory, J. N. Srivastava, ed., North-Holland, Amsterdam, 1973, 117–138.
  • [5] P. Erdős and R. L. Graham, Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory, Monographies de L’Enseignement Mathématique, No. 28, 1980.
  • [6] M. Filaseta, K. Ford and S. Konyagin, On an irreducibility theorem of A. Schinzel associated with coverings of the integers. Illinois J. Math. 44 (2000), 633–643.
  • [7] M. Filaseta, K. Ford, S. Konyagin, C. Pomerance and G. Yu, Sieving by large integers and covering systems of congruences, J. Amer. Math. Soc., 20 (2007), 495–517.
  • [8] R. Hough, Solution of the minimum modulus problem for covering systems, Ann. Math., 181 (2015), 361–382.
  • [9] R. Hough and P. Nielsen, Covering systems with restricted divisibility, arXiv:1703.02133.
  • [10] A. Schinzel, Reducibility of polynomials and covering systems of congruences, Acta Arith., 13 (1967), 91–101.
